chapters.title: Nothing left to do
chapters.description: After forgiving everything, the practice points to abiding with whatever remains when craving and hatred no longer need to do their work.

chapters.title: Breath and non-friction
chapters.description: The guided meditation deepens into natural breathing, softening, and a steady, low-friction attention that can rest in the body.

chapters.title: Everything tastes like Dharma
chapters.description: Awareness broadens to include reactions, longing, aversion, fear, hope, and even ordinary sounds as part of one continuous field of practice.

chapters.title: Rest in awareness
chapters.description: The closing inquiry asks what remains when the bell rings and whether there is an awareness that does not depend on effort, ending in simple resting.

title: Guided Meditation: Urgency of Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with fuller breaths and a slow settling into the body, then turns toward the subtle pressure behind restlessness: the wish to fix, achieve, heal, or make feeling go away. The practice keeps returning to sensation—especially the breath, the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and spine—while noticing how surges of feeling can create urgency without needing to be obeyed. Again and again, the talk points to a quiet alternative to control: relax where you can, absorb the impact of feeling, and let the breath become safe enough to return to.
short_summary: A guided meditation that works with bodily surges of feeling and the urge to control, using relaxation and breath to meet urgency more gently.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The meditation opens by softening the body through fuller breaths and a soothing exhale.

chapters.title: Relaxation cues
chapters.description: Attention moves through the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and a sense of brightness in the spine as the body yields to earth and sky.

chapters.title: Hidden agendas
chapters.description: The teaching invites noticing the semi-conscious agendas and efforts that keep trying to fix pain, peace, or the future.

chapters.title: Feeling as urgency
chapters.description: Brensilver describes how bodily feeling creates a sense that something must happen, and how to let feeling remain feeling.

chapters.title: False alarms
chapters.description: He frames sensations and phenomena as false alarms and emphasizes the difference between control and letting go.

chapters.title: Breath and equanimity
chapters.description: The practice returns to the breath, with repeated reminders to feel breathing in a relaxed way and to stay with each beginning, middle, and end.

chapters.title: Relaxing the threat
chapters.description: The closing section focuses on tension as an innocent control impulse and on relaxing the thorny quality of feeling until breathing feels safe again.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing and Letting Feeling be Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: When unpleasantness arises, can it simply be known as unpleasantness without needing to be fixed, explained, or turned into a story about who you are? The talk keeps returning to that question through a quiet guided practice of relaxing the jaw, belly, and mind; narrowing attention to the breath; and then widening it again when pain, fear, or restlessness need some room. Rather than asking you to push experience away, it moves toward a radical inclusion in which feeling can stay feeling, and the body can soften without pretending anything is absent.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing tension and meeting pleasant or unpleasant feeling without turning it into a story about self.

chapters.title: Know Feeling Simply
chapters.description: The meditation begins by naming pleasant and unpleasant feeling as they are, without needing to do anything else with them.

chapters.title: Relax the Urgency
chapters.description: Matthew repeats the invitation to let sensations mean less about identity, and notices how this allows the mind to relax.

chapters.title: Soften the Body
chapters.description: Attention turns to relaxing what can be relaxed, especially tension in the mind, jaw, and belly.

chapters.title: Feel the Breath
chapters.description: The guidance shifts toward the felt body and the natural rhythm of breathing, with subtle imagery used to support direct sensation.

chapters.title: Uncontrived Breathing
chapters.description: The breath is approached as unforced and uncontrolled, with tension understood as a cue for further softening.

chapters.title: Subtle In-Breath and Out
chapters.description: The in-breath and out-breath are noticed for their small effects of brightening and soothing, with attention to subtlety.

chapters.title: Widen the Aperture
chapters.description: The practice alternates between a small focus on the breath and a wider field that can hold pain, fear, fatigue, and confusion with mindfulness and love.

chapters.title: Radical Inclusion
chapters.description: The teaching states that Dharma does not ask us to pretend, but to include what is here and find a sense of belonging with experience.

chapters.title: Necessity of Love
chapters.description: Near the end of the sit, the talk points toward the simple dawning that love is necessary, and closes with a final reminder not to brace.

title: Guided Meditation: moving towards compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How can compassion be grown without denying the fact of suffering? The talk begins with a brief Issa poem about dukkha and then settles into guided practice: first softening the body and gathering the mind, then meeting heaviness, sorrow, and helplessness with a thoroughly undefended kindness. From there it turns toward the way love can be mixed with pain, supported by mindfulness, stillness, and the quiet wish that suffering lessen.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling and sensing suffering to letting love meet it, gradually orienting the mind toward compassion.

chapters.title: Opening and Issa poem
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the theme with a short poem by Kobayashi Issa and uses it to frame the coexistence of suffering and openness.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided practice begins by settling into posture and asking what conditions support love, including tranquility, collectedness, and temporarily putting the world down.

chapters.title: Awake to experience
chapters.description: Mindfulness is established as being awake to this moment, including sorrow, happiness, and whatever heaviness is present in the heart.

chapters.title: Meeting pain gently
chapters.description: He encourages an undefended relationship to suffering and invites imagery or silently repeated phrases that evoke care.

chapters.title: Love meets suffering
chapters.description: The talk names the meeting of love and suffering as a home for the Brahma-vihara and points to the universality of pain as a basis for wishing others suffer less.

chapters.title: Turning toward love
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the abrasiveness of cruelty and offers a grounded encouragement not to dismiss the reality of love because it is incomplete.

chapters.title: Body as antenna
chapters.description: In the quieter close, the body is described as a kind of antenna that can tune the whole being toward compassion.

chapters.title: Planting non-violence
chapters.description: The practice ends with resting, planting seeds of non-violence, and letting love stay close to the surface in ordinary moments.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: The final note returns to suffering as a cause for deeper understanding.

title: Guided Meditation: don't wait to let go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The meditation begins with posture, breath, and a felt yielding to gravity: finding both suppleness and strength, then giving the rest of body and mind to the earth. From there, the talk turns toward impermanence, suggesting that when we stop imagining ourselves as permanent, even one breath can feel enough; that same recognition becomes the basis for the refrain, “don’t wait to let go.” The practice movement is simple and direct—soothing the body, loosening grasping, and noticing how surrender changes the way everything appears. Near the end, the talk widens into loving-kindness and a brief personal reflection on grief and a friend’s death.
short_summary: A guided meditation on yielding to gravity, meeting impermanence directly, and letting go now rather than later.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: He begins by asking listeners to find posture, suppleness, and strength, moving into a quiet settling of attention.

chapters.title: Yielding to gravity
chapters.description: The practice emphasizes giving body and mind to gravity and the earth while exploring breathing that supports ease and aliveness.

chapters.title: Finitude and enough
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on impermanence, suggesting that seeing finitude clearly can make each breath feel sufficient.

chapters.title: Do not wait
chapters.description: The central refrain frames letting go as something to do now, with surrender changing how everything is seen.

chapters.title: Resting after surrender
chapters.description: A long stretch of quiet practice follows, holding the field opened by the instruction to release and return to breathing.

chapters.title: Practice loving
chapters.description: He notes that love is needed because the human condition can feel too much, and invites a simple abiding in loving attention.

chapters.title: More space around pain
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward the body-mind as a place where experience can be felt more directly, with less control and more room.

chapters.title: Remembering Kathleen
chapters.description: Matthew briefly remembers a friend who died that weekend, using the memory to speak about grief, claustrophobia, and the relief of more space.

title: Guided Meditation: Abiding in Care
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when kindness is not used as a strategy, but as a way of abiding? The talk moves through a guided settling into posture, softening tension and agitation, and letting love become an ambience of the mind rather than something performed or forced. From there, it opens into the tenderness of being human: the body, breath, grief, joy, and the way compassion can ease self-absorption without denying pain.
short_summary: A guided meditation that settles into bodily softness and uses kindness as a quiet, non-contending way to meet breath, feeling, and grief.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with an invitation to notice what the word meditation brings up and to find a stable posture.

chapters.title: Softening Tension
chapters.description: He guides repeated softening of the body, agitation, clinging, and rigidity, using kindness as the means.

chapters.title: The Heart's Realm
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the heart as a sacred realm where kindness softens self-view without needing to argue with it.

chapters.title: No Need to Perform
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes that love and kindness do not need to be used as a strategy against fear, but can become a quiet ambience of non-contention.

chapters.title: Finding a Support
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to choose an image, phrase, or bodily sense that resonates with care and lets the body ease into that tone.

chapters.title: Love and Spaciousness
chapters.description: The practice expands into the body and awareness, describing love as something that can make awareness feel less held and more open.

chapters.title: Four Forms of Love
chapters.description: He names love in relation to goodness, suffering, good fortune, and helplessness, then brings the practice back to the breath.

chapters.title: Breath and Presence
chapters.description: The guided meditation continues with breath, silence, and repeated reminders to stay with the felt sense of care.

chapters.title: Grief and Poignancy
chapters.description: The talk closes by relating grief to love, saying that care can soothe agitation while deepening the poignancy of being alive.

title: Guided Meditation: Renunciation and Dana
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if renunciation is not a harsh refusal, but a softening of the grip that keeps greed, aversion, and selfing in place? The talk moves from a simple meditation instruction—lay down greed and distress, as the Satipatthana says—into a gentler understanding of letting go as unclenching, relaxing, and releasing the anxious project of control. From there, it opens into generosity: when ownership loosens, life feels less like something to manage and more like gift, with breath, body, and daily life becoming forms of dāna.
short_summary: A guided meditation on renunciation and generosity that treats letting go as a softening into gratitude, goodwill, and the sense that life itself is gift.

chapters.title: Opening and theme
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a brief welcome and an invitation to settle around the themes of renunciation and generosity.

chapters.title: What to put down
chapters.description: Matthew frames renunciation as laying down what does not bring genuine happiness while keeping hold of what does.

chapters.title: Unclenching the fist
chapters.description: He describes letting go as a softening rather than a forceful rejection, more like unclenching than throwing away.

chapters.title: Greed and distress
chapters.description: The practice narrows to putting down the anxious project of life and the greed, distress, aversion, and grasping that keep it in motion.

chapters.title: Letting control soften
chapters.description: As the mind relaxes, he points to releasing the need to direct each moment toward a preferred outcome and using the moment as an invitation to let go.

chapters.title: Ownership and gratitude
chapters.description: He reflects on ownership as a knot of greed and hatred, then connects relinquishment with softening defensiveness, gratitude, and generosity.

chapters.title: Breath as dāna
chapters.description: The meditation continues to deepen into a quieter recognition that warmth and goodwill make generosity feel natural in the body and mind.

chapters.title: Life becomes gift
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Matthew explicitly recasts breath, body, and life as dāna, inviting a poignant gratitude for what is received and given.

title: Guided Meditation: Everything is Passing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when practice is seen not as a stable state to reach, but as something always moving? The talk begins with a gentle settling into breath and stillness, then widens into a meditation on reverence, care, lovingkindness, and forgiveness for our conditioning. From there it turns to impermanence: not just that things pass, but that everything is always passing, including the sense of self that seems to be watching it all.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling into breath and care toward a close look at impermanence, including the changing nature of the observing self.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: The meditation opens with welcome, a call toward stillness, and the first instructions for arriving in the body and breath.

chapters.title: Calling in practice
chapters.description: He invites a deep breath as a way of summoning all the moments of Dharma practice and the qualities they have cultivated.

chapters.title: Reverence and devotion
chapters.description: The practice shifts into reverence for the path, goodness, and love, with a sense of bowing toward what matters most.

chapters.title: Loving-kindness and care
chapters.description: He broadens into metta and a shared field of care, grounding the practice in kindness for all beings.

chapters.title: Understanding wandering
chapters.description: Attention’s movement away from the present is reframed as a kind of innocent, naive search for happiness rather than something to condemn.

chapters.title: Forgiving conditioning
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward forgiveness, softening judgment around conditioning and returning to breath.

chapters.title: The flow of anicca
chapters.description: Matthew develops the central teaching that everything is always passing, including the apparent stability of experience and the meditator’s sense of self.

chapters.title: Melting into breathing
chapters.description: The talk closes by letting the idea of a solid observer dissolve into the changing flow of breathing and direct experience.

title: Guided Meditation: Emphasizing Tranquility
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens if tranquility is less something we force than something we let settle? The talk keeps returning to that motion of easing off willfulness, yielding to silence, and noticing the restful texture already present in body and mind. It also names practical adjustments for tired or bright states, including how to work with agitation without demanding its absence. Near the end, the emphasis deepens into simplicity: as hunger in the mind subsides, less and less is needed, until just being alive can feel like enough.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, yielding, and noticing tranquility as something that emerges when effort softens.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a settling image from Thich Nhat Hanh and an invitation to let calm arise naturally.

chapters.title: Ceasing to Strive
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that relaxation comes less from doing than from stopping the urge to control the moment.

chapters.title: Yielding to Silence
chapters.description: He returns to the sense of silence drawing the mind inward, with a playful detour into a quoted line that is hard to place.

chapters.title: Finding the Quiet Beneath
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward yielding and sensing the silence beneath or beyond the noise of experience.

chapters.title: Brightness Or Rest
chapters.description: He adjusts the practice depending on energy level, suggesting either nurturing alertness or resting in subtle quiet.

chapters.title: Listening to Tranquility
chapters.description: After a brief pause in the guide, the meditation continues in the same spirit of open attention and receptivity.

chapters.title: Signs of Calm
chapters.description: Matthew notes that tranquility does not depend on total absence of agitation, only on noticing its signs.

chapters.title: Putting Down Burdens
chapters.description: The practice deepens into relinquishment, forgiveness, and the willingness to set down what can be released.

chapters.title: Enoughness and Closure
chapters.description: He closes by pointing to the mind's lessening hunger, Suzuki Roshi's reminder that being alive is enough, and the bell sounds that end the meditation.

title: Guided Meditation Presence is not a Transaction
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Start by settling the body well enough for a kind of truce with its imperfection, then let the breath and the closed-eye stillness signal that this moment is good enough to begin letting go. The talk keeps returning to a simple but demanding move: notice how the mind keeps propping up stories, fears, and control, and see the relief in setting some of that down. Midway through, the teaching sharpens into receiving experience in a non-transactional way—without trying to extract something from the moment—and trusting the moment as a teacher or medicine. It closes by widening the frame: meditation is not placed beside life, but life itself is held within the Dharma.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, releasing control, and receiving the moment without trying to get anything from it.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: He begins by inviting posture adjustments and a truce with the body’s inevitable imperfection.

chapters.title: Breath as Permission
chapters.description: The breath is used briefly as a signal to the deep mind that this moment is good enough to let go.

chapters.title: Stories Held Loosely
chapters.description: He points to the frameworks and stories that prop up a sense of self, noticing both their stress and the relief of putting them down.

chapters.title: Worry and Safety
chapters.description: The talk examines the fear that nothing can be released, and invites hearing, breathing, and resting without tracking threat.

chapters.title: Enduring the Practice
chapters.description: A long reflective pause follows, with brief reminders that even when practice feels hard, the path is still a support.

chapters.title: Receiving Without Taking
chapters.description: He names the core teaching move: receive the moment non-transactionally, trusting it as medicine or teacher rather than trying to extract something from it.

chapters.title: Life Within Dharma
chapters.description: The closing teaching reframes practice so that life itself rests within Dharma, with surrender deepening into wisdom and love.

title: Guided Meditation: Clinging is complicated, letting go simplifies
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Clinging is shown as tangled and effortful, while letting go is presented as simple, even if not always easy. The talk moves gently through settling the body, feeling the impact of hope, fear, and memory without finishing their stories, and resting attention in breathing. At one point, the breathing is framed as “whispers from the Buddha,” inviting a soft release into the present moment. The guidance keeps returning to a quiet balance: meet experience with warmth, and lay down just enough complexity to love and serve more deeply.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from the complications of clinging toward the simplicity of resting with breath, feeling, and gentle release.

chapters.title: Opening and posture
chapters.description: A brief welcome and invitation to settle the body and gather attention for practice.

chapters.title: Taking up practice slowly
chapters.description: He contrasts a gradual inner settling with the possibility of putting everything down at once.

chapters.title: Laying down complexity
chapters.description: The talk frames radical simplification as something that can support deeper love and service.

chapters.title: Hope, fear, and the body
chapters.description: Rather than following thought stories, the practice turns toward the bodily impact of hope, fear, and memory.

chapters.title: Breath as release
chapters.description: Breathing is presented as a quiet invitation to let go into the present moment.

chapters.title: Clinging and stickiness
chapters.description: He describes how auditory thought can feel sticky when there is little equanimity with feeling.

chapters.title: Gentle warmth
chapters.description: The closing section emphasizes the innocence of the mind and meeting it with warmth, simplicity, and care.

title: Guided meditation in Style of Shinzen Young: Mental Images, Auditory Thinking and Emotion in the Body
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How does a moment become suffering when sight, sound, feeling, and inner talk quietly harden into “me and my problems”? This guided meditation starts with settling the body, then moves through internal visual impressions, auditory thinking, and emotion in the body, showing how each sense gate can be noticed directly rather than followed. The practice keeps returning to a simple movement: hear thought as thought, feel feeling as feeling, and let experience unfold without taking it as a contract with the moment.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves through seeing, hearing, and feeling to show how selfing and suffering are assembled, and how they can be seen more clearly.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The practice begins by quieting together, easing the body, and finding a workable posture with some acceptance of discomfort.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and posture
chapters.description: The teacher names mindfulness as concentration, clarity, and equanimity, then invites a relaxed, upright embodiment.

chapters.title: Seeing mental images
chapters.description: Attention turns to the internal visual field, including fleeting images, visual thinking, and the sense of self carried in imagery.

chapters.title: Hearing inner thought
chapters.description: The practice shifts to auditory thinking, listening to verbal thought as sound rather than following its content.

chapters.title: Thought and orientation
chapters.description: The talk reflects on how language helps us track threats, opportunities, and time, and asks us to hear verbal thought without obeying it.

chapters.title: Feeling in the body
chapters.description: Matthew turns to emotion and affect as bodily sensation, noticing arousal, valence, craving, and clinging as changing physical patterns.

chapters.title: The three sense gates
chapters.description: Seeing, hearing, and feeling are gathered into one field of practice, with mindfulness meeting whatever appears and resting in blankness, silence, or peace when an object falls away.

chapters.title: Tracking the triad
chapters.description: A simple mental note of see, hear, feel is offered as support for moment-to-moment awareness across the three gates.

chapters.title: How suffering forms
chapters.description: The closing reflection investigates how suffering clusters around unnoticed sensory experience and how awareness can keep folding those patterns open.

title: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when meditation starts not with effort, but with a felt sense of goodness? The talk moves from goodwill and the “longing for less suffering” into a gentle practice of settling the body, narrowing attention without hostility, and resting with breathing, sound, and body as the foreground while everything else is left in the background. It then opens into a broader reflection on how trust in goodness can make the future feel less intimidating and make rest feel possible, even when care and responsibility have been running hard. Near the end, the talk turns to the need for cycles of love and rest, suggesting that always staying on duty can erode the very capacity one wants to preserve.
short_summary: A lightly guided meditation that begins with goodwill and goodness, then settles into resting attention and the need for balance between care and peace.

chapters.title: Opening goodwill
chapters.description: He begins by sensing the field of shared goodwill and the wish for less suffering.

chapters.title: Settling posture
chapters.description: The guidance invites a simple posture and a felt sense of sweetness and sangha in the body.

chapters.title: Goodness as practice
chapters.description: He frames Dharma as a gradual sensitization to goodness that brightens over time.

chapters.title: Trust and security
chapters.description: The talk turns toward trusting goodness, feeling less intimidated by the future, and resting more naturally.

chapters.title: Clear attention
chapters.description: He describes setting a gentle boundary around the meditation object and not making distractions into an enemy.

chapters.title: Resting now
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into resting with breathing, body, and sound, allowing the present moment to be enough.

chapters.title: Releasing memory
chapters.description: He speaks about a present that is easier to inhabit when the past and future are no longer pressing for analysis.

chapters.title: Care and rest
chapters.description: The closing reflection asks for cycles of love, devotion, and rest rather than constant caregiving strain.

title: Guided Meditation: If you're looking, you're lost
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by turning meditation away from effortful searching: rather than looking for a better state, the instruction is to find what genuinely inspires longing for the Dharma and then notice what serves in this moment. From there, the emphasis shifts to a very direct practice of inclusion—letting pleasure and pain be met more equally, and seeing how willfulness and doingness can quietly block the path. The closing movement is toward less and less strain: attention no longer needs to be propped up so aggressively, and awareness is described as continuing even when that effort is dropped.
short_summary: A guided meditation on moving from searching and willfulness toward a more relaxed, inclusive awareness.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens by welcoming the group and inviting a shared settling of body and mind.

chapters.title: Finding Inspiration
chapters.description: He suggests beginning with something genuinely inspiring that evokes longing for the Dharma.

chapters.title: Longing, Not Craving
chapters.description: The talk distinguishes dharma longing from the craving to get somewhere, then turns toward where attention is naturally drawn.

chapters.title: What Serves
chapters.description: Listeners are asked to notice the aperture of attention and choose an anchor in a way that fits the mind's present conditions.

chapters.title: Don't Go Looking
chapters.description: The instruction becomes more direct: don't look outside or even inside for something to find, because looking itself can obscure what is already present.

chapters.title: Radical Inclusion
chapters.description: Brensilver invites relinquishing the preference for pleasure over pain and widening the range of what can be held in practice.

chapters.title: Letting Go of Willfulness
chapters.description: He addresses the fear that goodness will be lost without effort, and gently questions the sense that more doing is still needed.

chapters.title: Awareness Needs No Job
chapters.description: The meditation closes by pointing out that awareness does not need to be propped up, and that the letting-go of effort can feel both sad and relieving.

title: Guided Meditation: Letting go of limitation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by settling the body and then gently asks what it would mean to put down ideas of limitation. From there, it explores how clinging makes the mind self-absorbed and narrow, and how a little less self-protection can open into safety, breath, and a sense of vastness. Later, the speaker turns to equanimity: not preferring the expansive over the contractive, and not trying to manufacture openness, but letting conditions soften on their own.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from grounding and safety toward loosening ideas of limitation, opening to space, and allowing equanimity to emerge without force.

chapters.title: Grounding and Settling
chapters.description: The meditation begins by stabilizing attention and finding something in experience that supports settling.

chapters.title: Putting Down Limitation
chapters.description: Matthew opens an inquiry into what it means to release ideas of limitation and the sense of a small, defended self.

chapters.title: Clinging and Vastness
chapters.description: He points to clinging as self-absorbing and suggests that loosening those habits can reveal space and a wider mind.

chapters.title: Safety in the Body
chapters.description: The practice turns toward enough inner safety to stop reiterating oneself and to melt into breath and body.

chapters.title: No Preference
chapters.description: Equanimity is framed as not preferring expansive states over contractive ones, which brings more space and stability.

chapters.title: Nothing to Manufacture
chapters.description: He emphasizes that this opening cannot be forced and leaves it to conditions and natural unfolding.

chapters.title: Samsara and Weariness
chapters.description: The talk later reflects on samsara as enchanting but eventually less compelling, where boredom can support letting go without aversion.

chapters.title: Closing Blessing
chapters.description: The meditation closes with a brief repeated blessing.

title: Guided Meditation: Grounding and Compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Begin by listening for silence, even when there is noise around you, and let thinking be received as just another kind of sound. The practice then settles into a quieter place, using an image of nectar sinking to the bottom of a glass to point to the body-mind unwinding into steadiness. From there, the talk turns toward compassion: first feeling the reality of suffering, then bringing someone to mind and gradually meeting their pain with warmth rather than defensiveness.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from listening for silence into settling, then into a direct practice of compassion for suffering.

chapters.title: Entering Silence
chapters.description: The meditation opens by resting in silence and noticing the play of noise and quiet around attention.

chapters.title: Thoughts as Sound
chapters.description: Matthew invites a relaxed relationship to thinking, treating mental activity as sound and noticing the quiet between thoughts.

chapters.title: Settling Like Nectar
chapters.description: Using Thich Nhat Hanh’s image of nectar settling in juice, he points to a deeper settling that may be true even when it does not feel pleasant.

chapters.title: Suffering and Gratitude
chapters.description: The practice turns toward the truth of suffering, the limits of what can be alleviated, and the gratitude that comes with recognizing precariousness.

chapters.title: Bringing Someone to Mind
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to picture a person who is suffering and to make their pain vivid and specific in the heart.

chapters.title: Taking It In
chapters.description: Matthew names the distress that can arise when we recognize another’s suffering and asks for a willingness to stay open to it.

chapters.title: Love in Response
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from empathic distress toward an expansive response of love and the wish for freedom from suffering.

chapters.title: Softening Into Compassion
chapters.description: The meditation lingers in repeated phrases as heaviness softens into warmth, with the final section marked by extended repetition that is partly unclear in the transcript.

title: Guided Meditation: Forgive all phenomena
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The practice starts by softening into posture rather than forcing anything, then turns toward the fatigue of always reaching for the next thing. The talk names samsara and craving as a repetitive habit of pursuit, even when it shows up as spiritual progress, and keeps returning to the simplicity of just one breath. In the guided section, the instruction becomes direct: forgive all phenomena, including pleasantness and pain, and trust the settling that follows.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from easing into posture to seeing the endless pull of “next,” then resting in forgiveness of all experience.

chapters.title: Opening Into Practice
chapters.description: A brief welcome and invitation to begin the meditation together.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: He guides a gentle way of arriving, taking time with posture and easing into practice without force.

chapters.title: Bored With Samsara
chapters.description: The talk reflects on weariness with praise, gain, and the effort to keep everything together.

chapters.title: The Pull of Next
chapters.description: Craving is described as the repeated movement toward the next thing, even when dressed up as progress.

chapters.title: Just This Breath
chapters.description: Attention narrows to simple awareness of the breath and the immediacy of this moment.

chapters.title: A Few Conscious Breaths
chapters.description: He emphasizes how quickly the mind can shift, even after only a few mindful breaths.

chapters.title: Forgive All Phenomena
chapters.description: The core instruction is to forgive every experience, including both pleasure and pain, and then let go of extra effort.

chapters.title: Resting In Ease
chapters.description: The meditation closes in a quieter, more open resting, ending with bell sounds.

title: Guided Meditation: Meditating on June 19
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with the question of where an anchor can rest—sometimes in the breath, sometimes in the body, sometimes as simple awareness itself. From there it moves into how the mind leaks into each moment and how practice can meet the surges, urgencies, and emotional currents of being human without adding more struggle. A Juneteenth reflection opens into a wider point: staying close to love by feeling the intensity of human life, the longing for peace, and a kind of radical nonviolence toward all phenomena, including the self. The meditation then settles into the image of peace at every sense door—body, eye, ear, and mind—as a quiet love at the gate of each experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from breath and body awareness into a Juneteenth reflection on love, nonviolence, and peace at every sense door.

chapters.title: Finding the Anchor
chapters.description: The guidance begins by exploring different possible anchors, from the whole field of awareness to the breath and the felt body.

chapters.title: Staying with Awareness
chapters.description: The instruction settles into repeated attention to awareness itself and the way the mind colors each moment.

chapters.title: Juneteenth and Freedom
chapters.description: Matthew names Juneteenth and reflects on cruelty, freedom, and the need to remain close to love.

chapters.title: Peace at the Senses
chapters.description: He draws on a traditional Buddhist description of enlightenment as peace at the body, eye, ear, and mind doors.

chapters.title: Stopping the Fight
chapters.description: The practice turns toward a felt release from self-conflict and conflict with the world, toward radical nonviolence.

chapters.title: Settling in Silence
chapters.description: A long stretch of quiet holding follows, giving space for the practice to deepen without much verbal instruction.

title: Guided Meditation: Following Interest
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with a quiet image of settling—like nectar sinking to the bottom of a glass—and uses it to explore what happens when meditation stops straining to hold everything in place. From there, it moves through the relief of not forcing attention, the fear that progress depends on control, and the invitation to trust that doing nothing can still be enough. It then turns toward a different relationship with awareness: following the thread of interest, letting curiosity animate practice, and offering attention as receptivity rather than as something used to get a result.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting attention settle, then following curiosity instead of trying to control or extract from the moment.

chapters.title: Settling Like Nectar
chapters.description: An opening image from Thich Nhat Hanh frames meditation as a settling rather than a struggle.

chapters.title: Letting Things Surface
chapters.description: The guidance describes how less suppression allows what is hidden or active to rise on its own.

chapters.title: Trusting Non-Doing
chapters.description: Matthew challenges the urge to control attention and emphasizes that nothing needs to be manufactured.

chapters.title: Following Interest
chapters.description: The practice turns toward the thread of interest, asking what this moment and this life are.

chapters.title: Curiosity as Practice
chapters.description: Curiosity and investigation are presented as a supportive intention, independent of where attention lands.

chapters.title: Receptive Attention
chapters.description: Attention is reimagined as a blessing rather than a way of extracting something from the moment.

chapters.title: Counting and Closing
chapters.description: The talk moves into extended counting, with the final stretch feeling like a quiet settling out.

title: Guided Meditation: Love and Dissolving Boundaries
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: This talk was given by Matthew Brensilver on 2025.06.04 at the Big Springs Retreat Center in Sierra City, CA. ******* Recording cut off due to technical issues ******* For more talks like this, visit AudioDharma.org ******* If you have enjoyed this talk, please consider supporting AudioDharma with a donation at https://www.audiodharma.org/donate/. ******* This talk is licensed by a Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-No Derivative Works 4.0 License
podcast_description: The talk begins by noticing how quickly life’s goodness becomes invisible, and how the mind shifts into constant fixing and optimization. From there, the practice turns toward appreciation for this body, this breath, and generosity, letting gratitude soften the tendency to see only problems. Renunciation is framed not as withdrawal, but as a kind of love: a quiet openness where urgency dissolves and nothing has to stick. The guided sequence then repeatedly invites softness, easing wherever the world seems to need you until only softness remains.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from gratitude into a very quiet, repeated softening practice, where letting go of fixing is presented as a form of love.

chapters.title: Appreciating good fortune
chapters.description: The talk opens by noticing how easily we overlook the many conditions that support our lives and bodies.

chapters.title: Beginning with gratitude
chapters.description: Practice starts by settling into appreciation for what has been given, including body, breath, and generosity.

chapters.title: Silent thanking
chapters.description: The meditation moves into repeated silent gratitude, allowing appreciation to deepen beyond words.

chapters.title: Renunciation as love
chapters.description: Matthew reframes renunciation as a dimension of love, where the urgency to fix begins to dissolve.

chapters.title: Quiet openheartedness
chapters.description: Love is described less as intensity and more as a steady, quiet openness that lets nothing stick.

chapters.title: Softening the world
chapters.description: The practice turns to softening wherever the world seems to need us, widening into a sustained instruction to soften.

chapters.title: Conditions well enough
chapters.description: The meditation closes by resting in the sense that conditions are already well enough, ending in a quiet thank you.

title: Guided Meditation: Embodiment and Allowing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: This talk was given by Matthew Brensilver on 2025.06.02 at the Big Springs Retreat Center in Sierra City, CA. ******* Insight Meditation Retreat: Beyond Judging, Comparing and Fixing (2025-05-31 00:00:00 -0700) ******* For more talks like this, visit AudioDharma.org ******* If you have enjoyed this talk, please consider supporting AudioDharma with a donation at https://www.audiodharma.org/donate/. ******* This talk is licensed by a Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-No Derivative Works 4.0 License
podcast_description: What does it mean to become available to this moment, rather than trying to force the mind into place? This guided meditation moves gently through relaxation, embodiment, and the feel of breathing in the belly and bones, while also noticing how relaxation can reveal aversion, vulnerability, and the limits of trying to manage experience with words. The emphasis stays on softening, opening awareness, and treating each moment as a fresh beginning, even when practice feels impossible. By the end, the talk points toward a freedom that does not announce itself, but is met by loving what arises without grasping.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing into embodiment, softening effort, and opening to whatever arises with less fixing.

chapters.title: Opening Intention
chapters.description: The meditation begins by asking what it means to be available to the Dharma and to this moment.

chapters.title: Willingness Over Willfulness
chapters.description: He contrasts a softer willingness with forceful striving and frames effort as alignment rather than control.

chapters.title: Relaxing into Newness
chapters.description: The practice turns toward relaxing more fully and meeting each moment as a fresh beginning.

chapters.title: Body as Refuge
chapters.description: Mindfulness of the body is emphasized as a way to keep presence steady while sights and sounds arise.

chapters.title: Breath into the Bones
chapters.description: Attention moves deeper into the belly and bones, with an invitation to live the moment less through conceptual thought.

chapters.title: Coping and Openness
chapters.description: He reflects on thinking as a form of coping and asks how much openness awareness can hold.

chapters.title: Softening the Impossible
chapters.description: When practice feels impossible or helpless, the instruction is to soften, keep the heart open, and remain available.

chapters.title: Freedom Without a Sign
chapters.description: The talk closes with images of freedom, emptiness, and loving phenomena so completely that the selfing around them can loosen.

title: Guided Meditation: Fresh Moment
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation starts by noticing how natural it is to expect the next moment to be more of the same, and then gently loosens that habit. The practice moves through relaxing what can be relaxed, settling into the body and breath, and meeting impermanence without being startled by it. Later, the speaker invites following whatever genuine interest arises, and then turns toward the freshness of not quite knowing, putting down expectations and sensing the quiet. The talk closes by asking what it means to cease reiterating the self and to simply breathe, feel the body, and rest in the moment.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting each moment freshly by relaxing expectation, following real curiosity, and resting in the quiet of not-knowing.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: A brief opening welcomes listeners and turns attention toward posture and beginning the practice together.

chapters.title: Not startled by change
chapters.description: The meditation frames practice as learning to meet impermanence without being jolted by it, softening resistance to the changing nature of experience.

chapters.title: Breath as soothing support
chapters.description: Attention turns to the felt experience of breathing as something gently holding and caressing the body.

chapters.title: Allowing the present moment
chapters.description: He encourages saying yes to the imperfections of this moment without making that a permanent endorsement, and describes each breath as a way of becoming more secluded and present.

chapters.title: Following genuine interest
chapters.description: When curiosity arises, he suggests treating it as a trustworthy thread for practice rather than trying to manufacture interest.

chapters.title: The aliveness of not knowing
chapters.description: The talk opens into the poetry of noticing familiar experience in slightly new ways and staying close to uncertainty without closing it down.

chapters.title: Releasing expectation
chapters.description: He invites listeners to put down expectations of finding themselves in the next moment.

chapters.title: Quiet and selfing
chapters.description: The meditation closes by asking what it means to cease reiterating the self, grounding that inquiry in breath, body, and quiet.

title: Guided Meditation: dropping secret agendas (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when meditation is driven by secret agendas, grasping, or the wish to make experience go a certain way? This guided practice keeps returning to a simple counter-movement: relax, stop looking for the Dharma, and let attention soften into breathing, body, and sound. The talk also turns to walking practice, where the work is to begin again and again, learning to stay calm, alert, and unforced while moving.
short_summary: A guided retreat meditation on relaxing control, releasing hidden wanting, and training the mind through receptive sitting and walking practice.

chapters.title: Settle and relax
chapters.description: The meditation begins with simple permission to relax and not go looking for anything special.

chapters.title: Hidden agendas
chapters.description: Brensilver names the subtle drives beneath practice—wanting to make things happen, make them stay, or control the shape of attention.

chapters.title: Wanting and attention
chapters.description: He describes how unnoticed grasping and aversion sponsor meditation and pull attention into the drama of gain and loss.

chapters.title: Let the self loosen
chapters.description: The talk turns to the meditator role itself, inviting the listener to let down the endless curation of self and receive the body, breath, and soundscape more simply.

chapters.title: Surrendering words
chapters.description: When difficult emotions and old fears arise, he points to the impulse to solve sorrow with words and suggests briefly releasing that habit.

chapters.title: Practice in the hall
chapters.description: The middle of the talk settles into extended guidance for sitting practice, emphasizing receptivity, awareness, and staying with experience as it is.

chapters.title: First and second arrows
chapters.description: Near the end he distinguishes unavoidable pain from the extra suffering added by resistance, while also acknowledging grief that cannot be dissolved.

chapters.title: Walking as training
chapters.description: He closes by explaining walking meditation as training in continuity, letting go at each turn, and carrying calm alertness into ordinary movement.

title: Guided Metta Meditation - Metta for All Beings (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if metta is not mainly a feeling, but a way of stepping out of self-judgment altogether? The talk begins by framing the Brahma-viharas as four forms of love, then moves through how loving-kindness can cultivate the heart, gather attention, and purify by bringing up what has not yet been loved. A long guided practice follows, starting with the body and breath, softening self-view, and widening toward phrases for oneself and then for all beings, even while the impossibility of ending suffering remains in view.
short_summary: A guided metta practice that moves from self-relation and inner quiet to wishing safety, peace, and ease for all beings.

chapters.title: Four kinds of love
chapters.description: Matthew opens by framing the Brahma-viharas as different ways love meets goodness, suffering, success, and helplessness.

chapters.title: Love changes self-view
chapters.description: He uses a retreat story to show how even a brief moment of metta can reorganize the relationship to self and loosen the self-as-project mode.

chapters.title: Measureless and quiet
chapters.description: The talk turns to the immeasurables and the reminder that loving-kindness may be subtle, quiet, and not defined by intensity.

chapters.title: Cultivation and purification
chapters.description: Matthew explains metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, including the way practice can bring buried grief or rage to the surface.

chapters.title: Settling into practice
chapters.description: The guided section begins with body, posture, and relaxation, then points to worthiness beyond self-judgment and the habit of proving oneself.

chapters.title: Phrases in the present
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of a mind made vast by love and uses simple phrases as a present-moment way to access the heart.

chapters.title: Resting in kindness
chapters.description: The practice widens through the recognition that whatever is arising can be held, whether by quiet rest or by bringing care to what has not yet been loved.

chapters.title: Love in hard places
chapters.description: Matthew names this as non-self-indulgent love and notes how tight spots can reveal the necessity of love that makes hatred less tenable.

chapters.title: All beings at ease
chapters.description: The meditation culminates in the wish for all beings to be safe, protected, and at peace, even while acknowledging that suffering does not end.

title: Guided Meditation: Non-referentiality
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The meditation starts by loosening the ordinary ways we orient ourselves: body, preference, craving, aversion, and the habit of continually locating who and where we are. From there, the talk repeatedly returns to the question of what it might feel like to be present without those reference points, using the phrase “without referentiality” as a refrain. The practice moves through settling, softening, and letting experience arise and dissolve without needing to pick anything up or define oneself by it, ending with a Rumi line about living in the “nowhere” you come from.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting with experience without using body, thought, or preference as fixed reference points.

chapters.title: Opening and arrival
chapters.description: Matthew opens with a brief basketball story and then invites everyone to settle in for the meditation.

chapters.title: Brightness and body
chapters.description: The practice begins by relaxing everything except brightness, filling the body with light, and letting the center of gravity drop into the belly.

chapters.title: Held by earth
chapters.description: He emphasizes gravity, the earth holding us fast, and fuller breaths through the belly.

chapters.title: Softening more
chapters.description: The instruction becomes simple and repetitive, encouraging a gradual softening into the sitting.

chapters.title: Reference points
chapters.description: Matthew uses the image of a phone pinging towers to explain how we locate ourselves through body, preferences, plans, craving, and aversion.

chapters.title: Without referentiality
chapters.description: The meditation shifts into a long refrain asking what it means to be here without reference points and to lay down sight, sound, sensation, and thought.

chapters.title: Endings and dissolution
chapters.description: Near the end, he invites tolerance for endings, things dissolving, and not needing to pick anything else up.

chapters.title: Nowhere you come from
chapters.description: A Rumi quotation deepens the final spaciousness, and the closing returns again and again to non-identification, including the meditator and the act of teaching itself as just another ping.

title: Guided Medtiation: Checking Hindrances, Checking Defilements
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The practice starts with a simple body landing, then moves into a careful scan of the five hindrances: restlessness, sloth, desire, aversion, and doubt. Rather than treating them as failures, the talk frames them as ordinary signals in the nervous system and invites a quiet “pulse check” of whatever is present. From there, the guidance keeps returning to how attention can get mixed with wanting, and how to soften into an unmixed awareness that bows rather than grasps. The talk closes by pointing toward surrender, with less effort to manufacture goodness and more willingness to let it be found.
short_summary: A guided meditation that scans the five hindrances and then softens into relaxed, unmixed attention.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens by landing in the body and establishing a steady, receptive ground for practice.

chapters.title: Scanning Hindrances
chapters.description: The teacher introduces the five hindrances and invites a simple check-in with whatever is present.

chapters.title: Bearing Unpleasantness
chapters.description: The focus turns to how unpleasantness is met in the nervous system and how awareness can hold it without judgment.

chapters.title: Choosing an Anchor
chapters.description: Different possible anchors are named, from breath and body to sound and open awareness.

chapters.title: Unmixed Attention
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes relaxing, receiving experience, and letting attention bow rather than grasp.

chapters.title: Letting Go Of Control
chapters.description: The talk points toward surrendering control and noticing that goodness does not need to be manufactured.

chapters.title: Putting Down Yardsticks
chapters.description: The closing movement gestures toward releasing comparison and measurement, and simply resting with what is here.

title: Guided Meditation: Softening the Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens if you stop trying to manage the mind and instead relax around it? This guided meditation begins with posture, then moves through softening, widening, and resting attention on the mind’s brightness without forcing it. The talk returns again and again to the felt knot of selfing, asking how much loosens when clinging, striving, and willfulness are allowed to unwind.
short_summary: A guided practice of relaxing, widening attention, and exploring how the sense of self softens when clinging is let go.

chapters.title: Arrive and Settle
chapters.description: The meditation opens by welcoming listeners, acknowledging the day's condition, and inviting a settled posture.

chapters.title: Relax into Brightness
chapters.description: He guides a full-body softening while asking the listener to stay tethered to the mind's natural brightness and luminosity.

chapters.title: Held by Love
chapters.description: The practice widens into a sense of shared presence, with repeated reminders to relax even the urge to do more.

chapters.title: Clinging and Letting Go
chapters.description: He names the strain of clinging, striving, and willfulness, then asks what remains when those habits are relaxed.

chapters.title: The Knot of Self
chapters.description: The talk shifts to the self as a knot of tension and explores how safety reduces trance and grasping.

chapters.title: Softening and Widening
chapters.description: Using the image of a snagging hangnail, he repeatedly invites softening and widening until attention feels more spacious.

chapters.title: Impermanence and Porosity
chapters.description: He brings in anicca and asks how deeply impermanence runs, pointing to a self that can melt, become porous, and fluid.

chapters.title: Closing Reflection
chapters.description: The meditation ends with one more invitation to notice how the self may be grasped or may open into something larger.

title: Guided Meditation: Arousal and Valence Signifying Nothing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Begin with the simple instruction to rest on something settled, then let attention notice how activation and intensity can coexist with a place in the field that is soothed and soothing. The meditation keeps returning to bodily arousal and valence—pleasant, unpleasant, and in-between—showing how craving runs on feeling and how feeling can be given space without being turned immediately into story. Over and over, the practice is to decouple sensation from the automatic loops of thought, let feeling signify nothing, and come back to the breathing until the ordinary reference points begin to loosen.
short_summary: A guided meditation that works with arousal, valence, and breath to let feeling be present without becoming a story.

chapters.title: Settling and Soothe
chapters.description: The meditation opens by anchoring to something settled and noticing that intensity can coexist with something soothing in the field of experience.

chapters.title: Pleasant and Unpleasant
chapters.description: He names the difference between subtle pleasant feeling and the stickier pull of pain, worry, and heartache.

chapters.title: Why Feeling Feels Safe
chapters.description: The instructions explore how feeling can seem protective and how the mind treats certain sensations as a basis for safety.

chapters.title: Tracking Arousal
chapters.description: Brensilver defines arousal and valence as body-level phenomena and links them to the felt texture of emotion and craving.

chapters.title: Giving Feeling Space
chapters.description: The practice widens into a larger field where feeling is allowed as much space as it needs, instead of being crowded into a narrow story.

chapters.title: Worth and Unconscious Feeling
chapters.description: He points to how questions of worthiness and value can arise from unconscious feeling and then repeatedly returns to clarity, equanimity, and alertness.

chapters.title: Tranquility Pulls In
chapters.description: A quieter phase develops as tranquility becomes more available and the body is guided to register feeling without forcing interpretation.

chapters.title: Decoupling Thought Loops
chapters.description: The core instruction emerges: when breathing is disturbed, practice separating arousal and valence from automatic loops of thought and letting feeling signify nothing.

chapters.title: Resting in Breath
chapters.description: The meditation settles into resting with whatever is pleasant in the breath, then continuing to stay intimate with breathing as the sense of self softens.

title: Guided Meditation: Surrendering the Territory of Me
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by narrowing attention to the faintest sound, then to darkness behind closed eyes, and then to the settled weight of the body. From there it turns toward the felt boundary of “me” — the urge to patrol, defend, and sort experience into mine and not mine — and invites a softer, more intimate relation to that contraction. The practice movement is toward laying down the fight, relaxing the territorial habit, and noticing that self and world are woven together in the same field of phenomena. By the end, the emphasis is on deep non-preference: whatever arises, even pain or densest selfing, can be met as another moment of offering the heart to Dharma.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from sensory settling into a gradual softening of self-boundaries, inviting non-preference for whatever arises.

chapters.title: Settling on sound
chapters.description: Attention opens with the faintest sound and the edge between hearing and silence.

chapters.title: Soft gaze inward
chapters.description: The practice shifts to resting visual attention in the darkness or brightness behind closed eyes.

chapters.title: Weight and ease
chapters.description: Instructions return again and again to grounded bodily sensation, heaviness, and simple restful alertness.

chapters.title: The guarded territory
chapters.description: Matthew names the sense of self as a protected boundary and invites recognition of the effort to patrol it.

chapters.title: Lay down your arms
chapters.description: The defense of self and world is softened, and the practice turns toward relaxing resistance.

chapters.title: Contracting and opening
chapters.description: He points to self as a contractive force and then to the expansive side where self and not-self are made of the same elements.

chapters.title: Life happening
chapters.description: The meditation broadens into allowing life to be experienced as happening, without centering everything on 'me.'

chapters.title: No preference
chapters.description: The talk turns toward loving preferences and control without taking them seriously, and toward peace that does not depend on phenomena.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: Matthew briefly cites Shinzen Young on non-preference, then ends by inviting an intuitive sense of self as a defended boundary.

title: Guided Meditation: The Pull of Time
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The practice begins by softening what can be softened, and then turns toward whatever tension, pain, or grasping remains without trying to force it away. From there, the talk moves through care for the body, the felt transmission of kindness, and a steady reminder that attention is often entangled with wanting and fear. A central turn comes with the recognition that we cannot control experience, so the practice is to let go and receive this moment; later, the talk widens that into a view that insight into one of the Four Noble Truths can be on offer in any moment.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from relaxing the body into releasing control, meeting tension with kindness, and opening to present-time insight.

chapters.title: Settling and Relaxing
chapters.description: The meditation begins by relaxing whatever can be relaxed and blessing what remains tense to stay as it is.

chapters.title: Tending the Body
chapters.description: Attention turns to bodily tightness or pain, with a suggestion to gently massage discomfort while staying in the flow of practice.

chapters.title: Care and Lineage
chapters.description: He frames self-care as an expression of loving-kindness and places the listener within a long lineage of love and care.

chapters.title: Practice and Human Longing
chapters.description: The talk widens to the wish to be free of suffering, the work of learning on the cushion, and the distinction between what is worthy of protection and what can be released.

chapters.title: Wanting and Fear
chapters.description: Brensilver reflects on how hope gets projected into the future and how renunciation loosens ownership, control, and our relationship to helplessness.

chapters.title: Letting Go to Receive
chapters.description: Using the phrase about not fooling Mother Nature, he emphasizes that we cannot control experience and must let go to receive this moment.

chapters.title: Insight on Offer
chapters.description: The meditation opens into a quieter teaching on recognizing what is happening, with the suggestion that insight can be available in any moment.

chapters.title: Not Missing It
chapters.description: The closing line returns to the simple task of doing our best not to miss the present opportunity.

title: Guided Meditation: Relax and Let go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Begin by settling the body and taking a few deeper breaths, then let the breath become less of a project and more of a place to rest. The talk moves from gentle posture instructions into a quiet argument for renouncing the impulse to solve life, with the poignancy of mortality helping that letting go feel possible. From there, the practice keeps returning to what is restful—the settled body, silence, the space behind closed eyes, and the soothing of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha—before opening into long stretches of simple counting and breath awareness.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses deep breathing, rest, and repeated letting go to soften the urge to solve everything.

chapters.title: Arriving to meditate
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a brief welcome and a turn into practice.

chapters.title: Posture and breath
chapters.description: Attention settles into the body, posture, and a few deeper breaths.

chapters.title: Letting settle
chapters.description: A Thich Nhat Hanh image supports the sense of the body sinking and settling.

chapters.title: Releasing the urge
chapters.description: Matthew turns toward renouncing the impulse to solve life and the freedom of surrender.

chapters.title: What feels restful
chapters.description: The instruction narrows to whatever is already restful, including body, silence, and refuge.

chapters.title: Resting with practice
chapters.description: The repeated invitation to rest continues, allowing the mood of ease to deepen.

chapters.title: Receiving the breath
chapters.description: The practice shifts back to the breath, including its beginnings and endings and the suggestion to place attention elsewhere if helpful.

chapters.title: Counting practice
chapters.description: The talk moves into a long sustained counting sequence, using repetition to steady awareness and reduce effort.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing the tension called 'me'
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when the tension called “me” is not argued with, but gently set down? This guided meditation moves from settling into posture and sensing the body to noticing how control is built through feelings, images, words, and subtle plans. The practice keeps returning to relaxation, surrender, and a simple wakefulness that can hold uncertainty without reaching for grip.
short_summary: A guided meditation that relaxes the body and attention while noticing how selfing, control, and uncertainty create tension.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: After a brief welcome, the practice begins by finding posture and arriving in stillness.

chapters.title: Touching Tranquility
chapters.description: He points to the muscle memory of Dharma practice and suggests recollecting tranquility and openheartedness.

chapters.title: Relax What Can
chapters.description: The instruction narrows to relaxing whatever can be relaxed while allowing remaining tension to stay.

chapters.title: Self as Headquarters
chapters.description: The talk turns to the sense of self as a headquarters for control, and surrender as the self laying down.

chapters.title: Seeing the Mechanics
chapters.description: He names the body, mental images, words, and auditory thought as the tools that organize clinging and control.

chapters.title: From Control to Wakefulness
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts from acquiring and strategizing to simply being awake, without a hidden agenda.

chapters.title: Contraction and Uncertainty
chapters.description: Near the end, he asks listeners to notice self-contraction as weight and to relax the uncertainty that fuels control.

chapters.title: Habitualizing Impermanence
chapters.description: The closing line gestures toward habituating to anicca as the practice continues.

title: Guided Meditation: Stillness and the Dissolution of the Predictive Mind
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you meet a mind that keeps leaning ahead into prediction? The talk begins as a guided settling: relaxing the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and breath, then resting in the simple pleasure of breathing and aliveness. From there it turns toward the subtle habit of expecting the next moment to be “me” again, and gently explores how meditation can loosen that predictive architecture and let attention fall into the present.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from deep bodily softening into a quiet inquiry into prediction, craving, and the release of “next.”

chapters.title: Opening and settling in
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group and invites everyone into practice with a quiet, communal tone.

chapters.title: Doing much less
chapters.description: He frames meditation as a response to life feeling overwhelming, a way of doing less and becoming open to experience.

chapters.title: Softening the body
chapters.description: The practice turns to releasing tension in the face, jaw, shoulders, and belly, with repeated reminders to soften and let go.

chapters.title: Grounding and openness
chapters.description: He includes the hips, pelvis, feet, and hands, emphasizing a sense of energy moving through the body without obstruction.

chapters.title: Breath and aliveness
chapters.description: The breath becomes the main object of awareness, and the talk settles into enjoying simplicity and the pleasure of breathing.

chapters.title: Predicting the future
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from bodily calm to the mind’s subtle habit of leaning forward with expectations and projections.

chapters.title: Samadhi and craving
chapters.description: Matthew describes samadhi as a dissolution of predictive architecture and connects craving with the word 'next.'

chapters.title: Falling into presence
chapters.description: He softens the assumption that 'I am next,' encouraging a direct return to the present moment and a release of self-making.

title: Guided Meditation: Much Becomes Incidental
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when you stop trying to build, manage, or improve experience, and simply listen? This guided meditation keeps returning to silence as something to attune to directly, even when noise and thought are still present. From that ground, pleasant and unpleasant, personal story, and even control itself are invited to become incidental for a little while, so the practice can rest in receiving the moment. The movement is toward surrender: no projects, no self-making, just space receiving space, with the suggestion that if anything is built, let it be love.
short_summary: A guided meditation centered on attuning to silence, letting preferences and self-concern become incidental, and resting in surrender rather than control.

chapters.title: Listening to silence
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting attention to silence itself, even when there is noise around it.

chapters.title: What becomes incidental
chapters.description: Pleasant and unpleasant feeling, autobiography, and fixed ideas are allowed to recede for a little while.

chapters.title: Receiving the moment
chapters.description: The teaching settles into the simple stance that receiving the present moment is enough.

chapters.title: No projects, no building
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes dropping the impulse to build, manage, or improve experience.

chapters.title: Surrender and love
chapters.description: Surrender is described as the dissolution of volitional energy, with love named as the one thing worth building.

chapters.title: Receiving space
chapters.description: The practice widens into a spacious resting in which space seems to receive space.

chapters.title: A new chance to let go
chapters.description: The talk returns to the ordinary urge to medicate feelings and thought, and frames the session as another opportunity to release control.

chapters.title: Emerging unchanged
chapters.description: The closing notes suggest that life will still be there afterward, the same and yet slightly different after practice.

title: Guided Meditation: Reverence & Renunciation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do reverence and renunciation meet in the actual moment of practice? The talk begins with posture, breath, and a sense of the ordinary becoming sacred, then moves into the idea that meditation is not just personal self-improvement but participation in a lineage of sincerity, wisdom, and compassion. From there, it turns to renunciation as a simple putting-down: of multitasking, worry, grasping, and the urge to make a story out of every sense impression, so breathing can become more intimate and life less complicated.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting practice with reverence and using renunciation to put down grasping, story-making, and mental clutter.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: A brief welcome and opening transition into shared practice.

chapters.title: Posture as Reverence
chapters.description: He frames posture as an open heart toward the world, with sanctity and relaxation held together.

chapters.title: Breath and Softening
chapters.description: The practice moves into the belly breath, encouraging a fuller and slower rhythm.

chapters.title: Sacred Lineage
chapters.description: He reminds listeners that even familiar meditation is part of a larger lineage of wisdom and compassion.

chapters.title: What to Put Down
chapters.description: Renunciation is defined as a simple, non-moralistic recognition of what can be set aside for now.

chapters.title: Story and Hope
chapters.description: He points to the tendency to proliferate around sense contact and to postpone life into the future.

chapters.title: Clinging Simplifies
chapters.description: The talk turns to the false promises of clinging and the relief that comes when grasping is released.

chapters.title: Resolution and Bells
chapters.description: He names samadhi as a momentary resolution of ambivalence, then the meditation closes in silence with bells.

title: Guided Meditation: All Phenomena are Friendly
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by asking what happens if we stop turning experience into a problem to be assessed, and instead let the body sit, breathe, and soften. It moves through posture, the pressure of self-making, and the way uncertainty can feel like too much responsibility, then uses the breath as a place to begin again without tension. Near the end, the phrase “all phenomena are friendly” becomes the main lens: not that everything is pleasant, but that experience can be met with less fear and less resistance, even when something hurts.
short_summary: A guided meditation that eases the pressure of self-assessment, settles into the breath, and ends with the refrain that all phenomena are friendly.

chapters.title: Arrival and settling
chapters.description: A brief welcome and orientation into practice, with an invitation to find a posture that balances alertness and relaxation.

chapters.title: Letting go of selfing
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward dropping the habit of assessing oneself and forgetting familiar stories about who we are.

chapters.title: Breath and fresh space
chapters.description: Guidance opens into breathing and bodily ease, encouraging a simpler relationship to the present moment.

chapters.title: Pressure to perform
chapters.description: Matthew explores how self-concern accumulates and how relaxing can soften the drive to prove, perform, or become.

chapters.title: Uncertainty and release
chapters.description: The talk names anicca and the burden of trying to manage every future self, then encourages resting in the open space of this breath.

chapters.title: Precision without tension
chapters.description: The meditation becomes more specific about the breath, with attention to beginnings, endings, and the pause of release.

chapters.title: All phenomena friendly
chapters.description: The central phrase is repeated and then clarified as a Dharma view: everything can be met as friendly, even when some experiences hurt.

title: Guided Meditation: Arriving and Opening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by asking for a kind of arriving that does not rush to force the heart or attention into a fixed shape. From there it gently scans posture, body image, self-consciousness, and places that hurt, inviting relaxation, softness, and a little patience with whatever is showing up. The middle settles into repeated breath awareness, then opens into a simple tension: the mind keeps foraging for scraps of peace, while the teaching points toward relinquishment and a satisfaction that actually nourishes.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling the body to breath awareness, then into letting go of clinging and finding a quieter kind of nourishment.

chapters.title: Arriving Gently
chapters.description: He begins by encouraging an unhurried arrival, without forcing posture or shaping attention.

chapters.title: Sensing What Needs Care
