chapters.description: The practice deepens into trusting that whatever arises can become love, with attention placed in the heart center.

chapters.title: Bathing pain in care
chapters.description: He acknowledges knots and pain in life and encourages meeting them with care, kindness, and an image or phrase that steadies the breath.

chapters.title: Lovingkindness phrases
chapters.description: The meditation shifts into explicit phrases such as “May I be well,” “May my heart be as wide as the world,” and “May I take refuge in love.”},{

title: Saturday Guided Sit
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A guided sit that begins by asking you to notice the heart’s inner posture and then ease off the habit of “doing” meditation. The practice moves through body awareness, the felt boundary between body and space, and the difference between arousal and valence—how feeling tones color experience without needing to become a story or action plan. From there, the talk turns toward receiving experience with clarity and equanimity, using even difficult sensations as invitations to let go and stay close to the path.
short_summary: A guided sit that centers body awareness, feeling tone, and the practice of meeting arousal and valence without turning them into a story.

chapters.title: Settling the posture
chapters.description: He begins by inviting attention to the body and the heart’s inner posture, then encourages less doing and more careful stopping.

chapters.title: Whole-body awareness
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward sensing the whole body, noticing what is often missed, and feeling the body’s edges and density in space.

chapters.title: Arousal and valence
chapters.description: He introduces arousal and valence as key dimensions of experience, including how emotions and simple sensations are colored by them.

chapters.title: Clarifying feeling
chapters.description: The practice focuses on mapping feeling in the body and recognizing that pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral states all arise within the same field.

chapters.title: Receiving without counsel
chapters.description: Rather than using feeling to make decisions, he invites being softened by experience and repeatedly returns to freedom as available in each moment.

chapters.title: The self-story loosens
chapters.description: He describes how affect can seem to issue orders and create a sense of self, then shows how awareness and disidentification make that grip easier to release.

chapters.title: Feeling as practice
chapters.description: The talk names the Buddha’s teaching that all dhammas converge on feeling and uses it to frame feeling as a field to honor, not always interpret.

chapters.title: Retreat and closing notes
chapters.description: He ends with practical reminders about staying held by the path, noticing habits, protecting silence, and meeting the rest of the retreat with sincerity and openness.

title: Thursday Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins by checking for fear, bracing, and the quiet apprehension that can show up at the start of practice, then meets whatever is there with kindness rather than pressure. From there, the talk settles into breath as an anchor: noticing where breathing is clearest, feeling how it can soothe agitation and enliven numbness, and letting attention widen through the whole body. It also names the mind’s innocent habit of moving into memory and anticipation, and invites a softer relation to effort, curiosity, and the pull to control. Near the end, the emphasis turns toward surrendering into the present and letting scattered intentions line up through release.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from meeting fear with kindness to settling with the breath, softening effort, and surrendering into the present.

chapters.title: Arriving with fear
chapters.description: The meditation begins by noticing fear, bracing, and the unease that can come with starting practice.

chapters.title: Common practice difficulties
chapters.description: Restlessness, sleepiness, doubt, and aversion are named as familiar conditions rather than signs of failure, with a brief reference to the Buddha.

chapters.title: Finding the breath
chapters.description: Attention is gathered on the breath at a chosen point in the body, with fuller breathing used to settle and clarify.

chapters.title: Breath through the body
chapters.description: The breath is explored as something that can soothe agitation, enliven numbness, and be felt through the whole body rather than only at one spot.

chapters.title: Willingness and curiosity
chapters.description: The instruction shifts from technique toward an attitude of willingness, curiosity, and interest in what attention and awareness are doing.

chapters.title: Grounded openness
chapters.description: The body is sensed as open yet grounded, with gravity and the belly becoming a place of stability.

chapters.title: Memory and anticipation
chapters.description: The mind’s movement into memory and future planning is described as innocent, along with the habit of using thought to stabilize experience.

chapters.title: Letting intentions align
chapters.description: The closing reflection notices background intentions, control, and aversion, then suggests that when we let go, the directions can come into alignment.

title: Formal Opening Part 1 (sit)
speaker: Ines Freedman, Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A short opening to the retreat, beginning not with teaching but with a 15-minute sit and the simple intention to bless the retreat by sitting together. From there the talk moves into practical setup: cushions, chairs, hearing devices, the daily rhythm of silent sitting and walking, and how instruction, talks, reflection, and practice discussions will be arranged. It ends with a gentle reminder that the week itself has been set aside for retreat, and that this shared time is already part of the practice.
short_summary: An opening retreat announcement that starts with a 15-minute sit and then lays out the daily rhythm, logistics, and practice structure for the week.

chapters.title: Opening the retreat
chapters.description: The retreat begins with a short invitation to sit and bless the time together through practice.

chapters.title: Comfort and seating
chapters.description: Practical guidance on hearing devices, cushions, chairs, and making the body more workable for practice.

chapters.title: Daily meditation rhythm
chapters.description: The basic rhythm is outlined: alternating silent sitting and walking meditation through the day.

chapters.title: Instruction and talks
chapters.description: He names the main touchpoints in the schedule, including morning instruction, the afternoon Dharma talk, and the evening reflection.

chapters.title: Practice groups
chapters.description: Participants are told how the discussion groups will work and that they will meet with both teachers over the course of the retreat.

chapters.title: Bell practice
chapters.description: Those ringing the bells are invited to practice beforehand, with a light touch preferred.

chapters.title: Closing encouragement
chapters.description: The opening closes with a check for questions and a simple appreciation for the time everyone has set aside.

title: Guided Meditation: Feeling and Verbal Thinking
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How can a meditation practice stay open when it keeps being pulled toward fixing, explaining, and controlling? The talk begins by questioning the impulse to reach for technique, then settles into feeling the body directly: its pressure, arousal, and shifting emotional textures, with attention and patience instead of defense. From there it turns to inner speech, treating thought as something that can be heard rather than obeyed, and finally to the way feeling and story shape each other. The practice movement is simple and sustained: notice, allow, and let both feeling and words be held in awareness with less compulsion.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from relaxing the urge to fix experience into close attention to bodily feeling and inner speech, so thoughts and emotions can be known without immediately following them.

chapters.title: Arriving and Settling
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes the group and begins by inviting a simple sitting practice.

chapters.title: Letting Go of Technique
chapters.description: He questions the urge to use technique as coping and asks what it is like to sit undefended.

chapters.title: Breath and Release
chapters.description: The practice shifts into fuller breathing and a sense of relaxing and cleansing the body.

chapters.title: Feeling in the Body
chapters.description: Attention turns to emotional and reactive sensations in the body, with emphasis on their location, intensity, and changing quality.

chapters.title: Patience and Permission
chapters.description: He encourages bathing feeling states in attention and patience, allowing them to move and self-liberate in their own time.

chapters.title: Hearing Thoughts
chapters.description: The meditation explores how thinking can be noticed as auditory experience, using Shinzen Young's teaching as support.

chapters.title: Feelings and Stories
chapters.description: He brings together words and feelings, showing how emotion can prompt stories and explanations that seem to stabilize experience.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Blessing
chapters.description: The closing invites equanimity with both bodily urgency and the mind's narration, ending in awareness, patience, and love.

title: Guided Meditation: You cannot know oneself while fixing oneself
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The meditation keeps returning to a simple refusal: not to turn practice into another project of fixing the self. It begins with posture, grounding, and the breath, then repeatedly lets go of willfulness in favor of surrender, silence, and the body breathing on its own. From there the talk widens into noticing fears, thoughts, and phenomena as empty and wisp-like, while meeting whatever arises with patience rather than control.
short_summary: A guided meditation on grounding, surrendering the project of self-fixing, and meeting thoughts and fears with patience and emptiness.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The meditation begins with posture, grounding, and a gentle sense of the spine and breath.

chapters.title: Breath and ease
chapters.description: Breathing is used to smooth the body-mind and create a steadier field of attention.

chapters.title: Putting down the project
chapters.description: Brensilver invites listeners to stop trying to fix life and to trust that help does not need to be hunted down.

chapters.title: Simple, beckoning practice
chapters.description: The talk narrows to the simplicity of body and breath, with a repeated sense that practice does not require willfulness.

chapters.title: Phenomena and fear
chapters.description: As thoughts and fears arise, the instruction turns to meeting them without freezing time or needing immediate reassurance.

chapters.title: Wisp-like experience
chapters.description: Experience is described as vivid yet attenuated, and the key line appears that if we only try to fix ourselves, we will never know ourselves.

chapters.title: Silence and patience
chapters.description: The closing instruction names silence as anchor and asks that every bit of noise be met with infinite patience.

title: Guided Meditation: Pervasiveness of Craving
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation starts with the felt tension between alertness and ease, using posture and breath to notice how craving and control create waves of strain. The practice then turns toward surrender: not trying to make experience exact, but relaxing into the life that is actually here, with its imperfection and heartache. Again and again, the talk asks whether awareness can be trusted to receive what comes and let it become wisdom.
short_summary: A guided meditation on noticing craving and control, then relaxing into the breath and the imperfect reality of this moment.

chapters.title: Settling Into Posture
chapters.description: The practice begins with posture, breath, and the balance between alertness and tranquility.

chapters.title: Noticing Holding On
chapters.description: He names craving, clinging, and the tension created by trying to control experience.

chapters.title: Surrendering to This Moment
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward accepting imperfection, letting go of self-shaping, and relaxing.

chapters.title: Breath as Pleasure
chapters.description: Attention to breathing is reframed as a simple pleasure rather than a task or test.

chapters.title: Responsibility Without Power
chapters.description: He reflects on the anxiety of feeling responsible for life while not fully in control of it.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: The meditation asks whether awareness can be trusted, while allowing coping without self-judgment.

chapters.title: Receiving Wisdom
chapters.description: In the closing stretch, the repeated invitation is to let this moment be received and trusted as wisdom.

title: Guided Meditation: Too Busy Knowing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when the mind stops patrolling experience and instead lets life be felt directly? This guided meditation starts with relaxing whatever can be relaxed, then asks for a deeper relinquishment of vigilance: assuming safety for a little while, surrendering to sensations, and using the breath, sound, or body as an anchor. The practice keeps returning to clarity and high-resolution attention, with impermanence as a cue to notice how experience snags and then lets go.
short_summary: A guided practice on relaxing vigilance, staying clear, and meeting experience moment by moment without holding it still.

chapters.title: Settling and Relaxing
chapters.description: The practice begins by relaxing whatever can be relaxed and noticing that softening is not about forcing anything away.

chapters.title: Safety and Relinquishment
chapters.description: He invites a temporary assumption of safety so vigilance can ease and the mind can surrender to what is present.

chapters.title: Anchors and Clarity
chapters.description: Breath, sound, the hands, or bodily pleasantness can serve as anchors while attention remains clear rather than foggy.

chapters.title: Snagged by Experience
chapters.description: Attention catches on sensations and phenomena, like fabric snagging on a hangnail, and the practice notices that snagging without panic.

chapters.title: Moment by Moment Knowing
chapters.description: Impermanence is brought forward as the mind learns to know experience in motion instead of holding it still.

chapters.title: Too Busy Knowing
chapters.description: The talk ends on the idea that intimate knowing can be so continuous that it leaves little room for a fixed identity.

title: Guided Meditation: Sitting in the Bones
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The practice begins by softening the breath and letting attention settle lower, as if awareness can pour down into the body rather than stay caught in the head. From there, the talk keeps returning to the felt sense of sitting in the bones: heaviness without dullness, groundedness, steadiness, and a trust that the body can simply rest in gravity. That quiet stability opens into a wider invitation to stop doing, relax the small self, and let life arise and cease with more ease. Near the end, the emphasis turns toward love and metta, suggesting that even a tranquil body can become a way of radiating loving-kindness.
short_summary: A guided practice in settling into the body, relaxing effort, and letting groundedness open into metta.

chapters.title: Settling the Breath
chapters.description: Fuller breathing is used to soften the belly and signal the body that it can relax.

chapters.title: Sitting in the Bones
chapters.description: The instruction turns to feeling the body as grounded and heavy, with attention dropping below the head.

chapters.title: Gravity and Earth
chapters.description: He emphasizes the downward pull of gravity and the sense of the earth offering support.

chapters.title: Attention Downward
chapters.description: When awareness drifts upward, the instruction is to pour it back into the body and let it seem to arise from the belly.

chapters.title: Mountain Stability
chapters.description: The practice is framed as growing rootedness, with a brief reference to Chögyam Trungpa's mountain image.

chapters.title: Stop Doing So Much
chapters.description: Relaxation is presented as a release from strain and self-effort, trusting awareness to remain awake without constant doing.

chapters.title: The Self Can Relax
chapters.description: The sense of a contracted 'me' is invited to loosen, making room for a wider, less possessive awareness.

chapters.title: From Rest to Metta
chapters.description: Steadiness is linked with love, and the talk ends by folding loving-kindness into the practice as something we may need even when we do not feel inclined toward it.

title: Guided Meditation: Landing in the Breath and Soundscape
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Big Bear Retreat Center) These recordings are available publicly from a retreat held in person at Big Bear Retreat Center. Learn more about the center and upcoming offerings for retreats in nature, gathered in community. bigbearretreatcenter.org/upcoming-retreats/ Healing the Self, Loving the Self, Forgetting the Self Matthew Brensilver July 9 - 13, 2024 Co-sponsored by Insight Retreat Center (IRC) and Big Bear Retreat Center In this silent retreat, we explore how mindfulness supports the healing of the self. Many of the Buddhist teachings help us to tend to painful memory, old pains and the habits that compound suffering. As we become more gentle and loving towards experience, the self becomes less and less of a preoccupation. The more completely we accept ourselves, the easier it becomes to forget the self and rest in an awareness unencumbered by self-consciousness. This retreat includes sitting and walking meditation instructions, and dharma talks.
podcast_description: Fear is treated as the first thing to meet here: the talk begins by naming how bracing, worry, and the urge to get through practice can narrow the body and mind. From there it moves into a quiet sequence of instructions—softening with kindness, settling into the breath, opening to sound, and learning to stay awake through boredom, overwhelm, or even a “meltdown” without turning experience into a problem. Walking practice becomes a place to rotate attention across seeing, hearing, the feet, the whole body, and even love, with the repeated reminder that willingness matters more than force.
short_summary: A guided meditation on softening fear, resting attention in breath and sound, and staying gently awake through the ordinary textures of retreat.

chapters.title: Settling fear
chapters.description: He opens by naming fear as a deep shaping force and invites kindness toward any bracing or anxiety present.

chapters.title: Mood in awareness
chapters.description: The instruction turns to seeing background mood directly, letting it be held in awareness rather than filtering experience through it.

chapters.title: Breath as refuge
chapters.description: He guides attention to the breath in a precise but relaxed way, emphasizing release at the end of the exhale and trust in the body.

chapters.title: Thinking without struggle
chapters.description: Matthew explores the mind’s habit of strategizing and securing itself through thought, and suggests using awareness instead of words for refuge.

chapters.title: Awake and relaxed
chapters.description: He softens the approach further, contrasting alertness with defensiveness and inviting a simple, steady attention.

chapters.title: Listening to soundscape
chapters.description: For those for whom the breath is not enough, he offers sound and silence as another anchor, including nearby, distant, and ending sounds.

chapters.title: Retreat rhythm
chapters.description: The talk shifts into practical retreat guidance about how the first day can feel swampy, why it helps to normalize difficulty, and how sitting and walking support each other.

chapters.title: Whole-day awareness
chapters.description: He draws on Sayadaw Tejaniya and a rotating set of anchors to encourage broad, continuous awareness, including boredom, simple sensation, and the possibility that practice changes us quietly over time.

chapters.title: Closing encouragement
chapters.description: Matthew closes with practical reminders about pacing, practice meetings, and letting both ease and discomfort soften the heart.

title: Guided Meditation: Non-tension
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you stop fighting conditions that are already here? This guided meditation works with discomfort, bracing, and the urge to wait for things to be better, inviting a simple release into whatever is present. The practice moves through sensing the body, softening tension, and letting awareness settle more fully into change and impermanence. It ends with a repeated image of falling into the present, with less baggage and fewer places for attention to snag.
short_summary: A guided meditation on releasing tension by giving permission to the present moment, softening against impermanence, and falling more deeply into awareness.

chapters.title: Opening and welcome
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes listeners and introduces the practice with a light, informal tone.

chapters.title: Temperature and the body
chapters.description: He recalls a teacher’s reminder that even the Buddha sweats and shivers, using discomfort to point toward direct observation.

chapters.title: Not waiting for perfect
chapters.description: The talk turns to letting imperfection become motivation to stop fighting and to give permission for the moment to be as it is.

chapters.title: Awareness in the body
chapters.description: Listeners are guided to fill the body with awareness in a way that feels expansive rather than forced.

chapters.title: Relaxing clinging
chapters.description: He names clinging as a source of tension and invites a settled, undefended aliveness.

chapters.title: Impermanence as practice
chapters.description: Matthew links attention with change and encourages not pushing or pulling on impermanence or seeking escape from it.

chapters.title: Falling into presence
chapters.description: The closing refrain uses the image of falling deeper into the present, with less baggage and fewer ways for attention to catch.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing and Falling Awake
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with a simple instruction: find a posture that balances relaxation and alertness, then notice what is still carrying the day. The practice moves through feeling the residue of stress, offering it a blessing rather than tightening around it, and returning to the exhale, faint sounds, and the felt edge between sound and silence. Over and over, the talk points to the same turn: relaxing not by force, but by softening the willfulness that keeps holding on.
short_summary: A guided practice in softening the grip of tension, blessing what remains, and settling toward openness rather than control.

chapters.title: Settling Into Posture
chapters.description: He begins by inviting a balanced posture that tilts toward alertness or relaxation depending on what is needed.

chapters.title: Laying Down the Day
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward whatever is still entangling from the day or week, with encouragement to feel and bless it without getting more caught.

chapters.title: Blessing and Release
chapters.description: He emphasizes giving blessing to what is present so it can be set down rather than carried forward.

chapters.title: Breath and Sound
chapters.description: Attention opens to the ends of exhalations and to very faint sounds, including the edge between hearing and silence.

chapters.title: Tension and Holding
chapters.description: The talk names greed, hatred, selfing, and duality as forms of tension, and contrasts them with the limits of willful relaxation.

chapters.title: Relinquishing Control
chapters.description: He describes a faith-filled softening in which the mind stops trying to engineer experience and lets the moment do some of the work.

chapters.title: Relaxing as Exhale
chapters.description: The meditation continues with a quieter settling around the felt sense of relaxing, leading into a brief reflective turn on the practice itself.

chapters.title: Falling Awake
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting commanded relaxation with genuine relinquishment, comparing it to falling asleep and suggesting that awakening can happen from openness.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxation and the Tension of Dukkha, Self, Clinging
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if the way into practice is not more effort, but a deeper and more honest relaxing? The talk moves from a small image of a tired child asking for the bed that is already there, into a sustained inquiry into how clinging, selfing, greed, hatred, and delusion show up as tension in the body-mind. Through breath, gravity, and repeated softening, the speaker suggests that when attention keeps releasing its grip, what has been held by suffering can finally be felt and let go.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing into the body and noticing how clinging, selfing, and other forms of suffering register as tension.

chapters.title: Opening story
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a family story that turns into a playful question about where awareness and the Dharma are found.

chapters.title: Relaxation and suffering
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of awakening through deep relaxation and names tension as a signature of greed, hatred, selfing, and suffering.

chapters.title: Right here to sit
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the immediacy of practice, with the reminder that the relief we seek may be close at hand.

chapters.title: Let tension speak
chapters.description: He suggests allowing tension permission to be present, while using breath and awareness to invite ease rather than struggle.

chapters.title: Gravity and ease
chapters.description: The guidance shifts into body-based support, especially the soothing downward pull of gravity and an upright but released posture.

chapters.title: Self as contraction
chapters.description: Matthew explores selfing as a dense, contractive force and invites a relinquishing of ownership, proving, and defensiveness.

chapters.title: Clinging and hate
chapters.description: He describes clinging as complicated but simplifying when released, then turns to why hatred cannot ultimately work.

chapters.title: Delusion and mortality
chapters.description: The meditation widens to delusion, disappointment, and the turning toward death as a way of softening attention.

chapters.title: Putting things down
chapters.description: Drawing on Shinzen Young's phrase, Matthew closes by encouraging continued softening until awareness feels weightless.

title: Guided Meditation: Self and Willfulness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by asking what it means to start meditating at all, then quickly turns toward a harder question: can you stop doing the many things you think you need to do? From there, the practice keeps loosening the grip of willfulness, inviting trust in awareness, in the body, and in what is already present without being managed. Rather than building a technique, the guidance repeatedly asks the listener to put efforts down, notice suspicion of self, and let doing dissolve into openness.
short_summary: A guided meditation that explores how to release willfulness and trust awareness without trying to control it.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a brief welcome and settles into the instruction to meditate.

chapters.title: What Began Meditation
chapters.description: He asks listeners to look at what they do when they first begin meditating, then to notice the impulse to stop doing.

chapters.title: Trusting the Mind
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the suspicion that we must make something happen, and toward trusting mind and awareness instead.

chapters.title: The Self as Organizer
chapters.description: He reflects on how even simple attentional instructions can give rise to a self that orchestrates practice.

chapters.title: Anchor Everywhere
chapters.description: The teaching widens the sense of support, suggesting that what we trust may not depend on personal effort alone.

chapters.title: Willfulness and Job
chapters.description: He names willfulness as part of the self's work and questions the role we keep assigning ourselves in meditation.

chapters.title: Awareness Finds Us
chapters.description: The practice deepens into letting awareness meet us, rather than reaching for it through effort.

chapters.title: Surrender and Ease
chapters.description: The closing settles into surrender, asking practice to become a movement of trust and release.

title: Guided Meditation: Tranquility
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you let the mind settle without forcing it, and then use that settling to see more clearly? This guided meditation moves gently through the body and breath, asking you to elongate the breathing, notice what is already resting, and let tension drain rather than be managed by effort. From there it opens into listening for silence, resting with the visual field behind closed eyes, and softening the habit of clinging long enough to return to stillness.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses breath, body, silence, and relaxed attention to cultivate tranquility as a way of seeing more clearly.

chapters.title: Tranquility and clarity
chapters.description: He introduces tranquility as both a calming quality and a way of clarifying the inner landscape.

chapters.title: Resting the body
chapters.description: The practice opens with acknowledging fatigue, lengthening the breath, and sensing into what is already resting in the body.

chapters.title: Softening clinging
chapters.description: He invites releasing tension and meeting grasping, aversion, and control with a gentler breath and a wider field.

chapters.title: Body as a resource
chapters.description: Repeated cues to soften and widen lead into finding a natural bodily zone that can serve as an anchor.

chapters.title: Listening for silence
chapters.description: Attention shifts from breath to silence, including distant sounds and the space around sound, as a live quality rather than the absence of noise.

chapters.title: Silent listening
chapters.description: Mother Teresa's line about prayer frames a contemplative listening that can be practiced without any religious belief.

chapters.title: Visual field and darkness
chapters.description: He turns to the visual field with closed eyes, noticing brightness, images, and the darkness behind them while resting attention gently.

chapters.title: Letting clinging wait
chapters.description: Near the close, he names the impulse to cling and suggests promising it a return later, not now.

chapters.title: Closing rest
chapters.description: The meditation ends in sustained quiet, with repeated gentle cues and little further instruction.

title: Guided Meditation: Goodnight Moon
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: A children’s bedtime book becomes the frame for this meditation: the repeated act of saying goodnight to memories, pain, thoughts, feelings, and plans. The talk moves gently from that image into a guided sitting, with deep breaths, a soothing out-breath, and a practice of putting down whatever has been lingering from the day or week. It keeps returning to the question of how to release experience without bracing against it, so what remains can rest in a wider, quieter field.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses the rhythm of Goodnight Moon to practice gently putting down thoughts, feelings, and lingering pain.

chapters.title: Opening the Meditation
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes the group and introduces the evening’s meditation, then recalls Goodnight Moon as the image that will shape the practice.

chapters.title: Saying Goodnight
chapters.description: He turns the book’s repeated refrain into an instruction to bless and release memories, pain, thoughts, feelings, and other pieces of experience.

chapters.title: Settling With Breath
chapters.description: The practice shifts toward breathing as a steady place to land, with attention and awareness described as gentle and open.

chapters.title: Open Awareness
chapters.description: He explores how the breath can be felt either within attention or arising in spaciousness, and encourages bidding goodnight to whatever can be put down.

chapters.title: Not Rejection
chapters.description: Matthew clarifies that putting something down is not the same as exile or aversion, and points toward patient, non-grasping attention.

chapters.title: Resting in Presence
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into a quiet surrender of effort, with an emphasis on placing hope in this moment and allowing awareness to hold what remains.

chapters.title: Fatigue and Brightness
chapters.description: Near the end, he includes fatigue itself as something that can soothe the body, while awareness leaves a brightness in its wake.

title: Guided Meditation: Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens if feeling is not treated as a problem to fix, but as the field where reactivity reveals itself and can unwind? This guided meditation begins with breath and bodily sensation, then moves through the way feeling shapes sleepiness, restlessness, craving, and doubt, inviting a simpler seeing that does not keep narrating everything into a story. From there, the practice turns toward relinquishment, non-thinking, and a quieter kind of effort—one that can rest in the present moment without forcing it.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses breath, feeling, and non-conceptual awareness to loosen clinging and the habit of compulsively solving experience.

chapters.title: Settling With Breath
chapters.description: The meditation begins with exhalation, body awareness, and a gradual settling into the retreat sit.

chapters.title: Problems and Dissolving
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts problems that need solving with problems that dissolve when held in awareness.

chapters.title: Commitment To This Moment
chapters.description: He frames practice as a commitment to stay with what is here rather than keep escaping into fixing.

chapters.title: Wanting And Knowing
chapters.description: The talk explores how wanting shapes knowing, and how relinquishment changes what perception can reveal.

chapters.title: Feeling And Reactivity
chapters.description: He names common reactions like sleepiness, agitation, craving, ill will, and doubt as movements rooted in feeling.

chapters.title: Stories From Feeling
chapters.description: Matthew describes how we keep narrating experience, and invites feeling to be just feeling without making a story.

chapters.title: Non-Thinking And Release
chapters.description: He distinguishes thinking from coping and encourages non-conceptual awareness supported by the breath.

chapters.title: Clinging And Heaven
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward letting go of clinging and noticing how easily the mind can cross into suffering.

chapters.title: Futurelessness And Death
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the thin line between heaven and hell, the heaviness of the future, and how death clarifies what matters.

title: Guided Meditation: Convergence of Insight and Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How does mindfulness open into love, and how does love make mindfulness bearable? This guided meditation moves steadily from the careful deconstruction of experience into the four Brahma-viharas—loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity—showing how each one meets a different face of daily life. The practice then turns inward with simple offerings and phrases like “May I be happy” and “May my heart be softened rather than hardened by difficulty,” letting the listener rest in a wider, less self-referential heart.
short_summary: A guided meditation on how insight and the four Brahma-viharas meet in practice, moving from careful attention to simple heart-based offerings and phrases.

chapters.title: Insight and love
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the tension and overlap between mindfulness, wisdom, and loving-kindness, then shows why opening to experience requires trust and care.

chapters.title: The four abodes
chapters.description: He names the brahma-viharas as resting places of the heart and briefly maps each one to a different situation in practice and daily life.

chapters.title: Compassion and equanimity
chapters.description: The talk lingers on compassion as meeting suffering honestly, then on equanimity as remaining open when suffering cannot be fixed.

chapters.title: Letting love be home
chapters.description: He emphasizes that this path is not about pretending, but about discovering a love that feels natural and already near.

chapters.title: Dropping specialness
chapters.description: The guided meditation turns toward self-view, inviting listeners to stop comparing, stop measuring, and meet experience without making the self a special case.

chapters.title: Offering goodwill
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to sense what kind of love is needed, notice hurt and sincerity, and offer phrases like 'May I be happy' and 'May I be softened.'

chapters.title: Softening into the heart
chapters.description: The closing instructions deepen the practice of loving-kindness and acceptance, including space for grief, fear, and the boundlessness of the heart.

title: Guided Meditation: The Breath and Awareness don't depend on you
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by shifting attention away from self-management: the breath is emphasized because it does not depend on what we call “me,” and awareness, too, is presented as something that cannot be started or stopped by will. From there, the practice settles into a long, quiet emphasis on emptiness, with repeated returns to the sense that experience is rising and passing without a central controller. A brief reference to stepping out of the “bureaucracy of ego” gives the teaching an image of leaving behind the micromanagement of self and resting in a more open, effortless seeing.
short_summary: A guided meditation on breath and awareness as impersonal, effortless, and empty of the need to be controlled by “me.”

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: Opening remarks, a brief schedule note, and an invitation to arrive and practice together.

chapters.title: Breath Beyond Self
chapters.description: The meditation begins by emphasizing that breathing does not depend on the constructed sense of me or on effortful control.

chapters.title: Awareness Receives
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from the breath to awareness, pointing out that attention and knowing also do not depend on personal will.

chapters.title: Emptiness Repeated
chapters.description: A long contemplative stretch returns again and again to emptiness, letting the phrase do the work of loosening fixation.

chapters.title: Ego's Bureaucracy
chapters.description: Matthew cites Trungpa Rinpoche to describe insight as stepping out of the bureaucracy and micromanagement of ego.

chapters.title: Same Stuff, Rising
chapters.description: He expands on self-related phenomena as part of the same flux as everything else, marked by the openness of awareness and the effortlessness of breathing.

chapters.title: Egoic Proprioception
chapters.description: Using the body’s balance sense as a comparison, he describes how the ego keeps tabs on itself and continually re-creates a sense of place.

chapters.title: Less and Less Me
chapters.description: The closing movement suggests that, as sensitivity grows, the self stops feeling like a home base and other modes of awareness come online.

title: Guided Meditation: Awareness and Effortlessness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How does meditation move from effort into a silence that is already there? The talk begins with the mind’s habit of turning experience into a story about me, then uses a contemporary philosopher’s description of awareness as silent, non-reactive, and without inner agency to point toward a more immediate sense of knowing. From there, the guidance turns practical: breathe, relax what can be relaxed, soften friction with imperfection, and see how effort, choicelessness, and non-doing can gradually give way to trust and rest.
short_summary: A guided meditation on loosening narrative, softening friction, and letting effort settle into effortless awareness.

chapters.title: Narrative Self
chapters.description: He begins by noticing how meditation is easily folded into the story of who we are and what is happening.

chapters.title: Silent Knowing
chapters.description: Using Thomas Metzinger as a reference, he describes awareness as silent, non-conceptual, and free of grasping.

chapters.title: Instructions And Fruit
chapters.description: The meditation instructions are framed as gestures toward qualities that become natural when effort drops away.

chapters.title: Settling To Sit
chapters.description: He invites the group to sit, breathe, and relax whatever can be relaxed.

chapters.title: Friction And Equanimity
chapters.description: The teaching turns to frictionlessness, imperfection, and equanimity as a way of not fighting experience.

chapters.title: Effort Toward Release
chapters.description: He emphasizes that some effort may be needed at first in order to relinquish willfulness and choice-making.

chapters.title: Trusting Silence
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a quieter trust, where the moment stops feeling like a task and silence feels more central than sound.

chapters.title: Resting Safely
chapters.description: In the closing portion, he says life and awareness do not depend on personal control, and ends by noting the intended audience for these sessions.

title: Guided Meditation: Language as Safety
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How much of what we call safety is really just the habit of using words to manage experience? The talk begins by questioning the common belief that thinking is the problem, and turns instead to an over-trust in language as a way of fixing unpleasantness. From there, the guided meditation asks for a sustainable posture, relaxed attention, and a willingness to rest, feel the breath, and absorb discomfort without immediately telling a story about it. By the end, the movement is toward a quieter safety: not certainty or verbal control, but wisdom, love, and the ability to stay with uncertainty.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the urge to fix experience with words, and finding steadier safety in silence, feeling, and awareness.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: He introduces the talk’s central idea: meditation is not mainly about stopping thought, but about softening our over-trust in language.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The practice begins with posture, relaxation, and permission for imperfection before the deeper meditation unfolds.

chapters.title: Releasing urgency
chapters.description: He explores how relaxation reduces the urge to fix unpleasantness and helps prepare the heart for inevitable imperfection.

chapters.title: Rest without words
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward simple resting and noticing how quickly the mind reaches for words to soothe feeling.

chapters.title: Breath and feeling
chapters.description: Attention is brought to breath as direct sensation, with images and inner commentary gently set aside.

chapters.title: Uncertainty and story
chapters.description: He asks how much uncertainty we can tolerate before the mind begins to build a story, and distinguishes thought from the experience it tries to manage.

chapters.title: Letting go into silence
chapters.description: The teaching lands on the idea that language may not be making us safer in this moment, so the practice is to release into feeling and silence.

chapters.title: Risk and deeper safety
chapters.description: In the closing movement, he names the paradox that change can feel risky while words feel safe, and points to wisdom and love as the deeper refuge.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention, Effort, Choice and Selfing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do attention, effort, choice, and selfing fit together in meditation? The talk begins by naming the familiar work of practice—directing attention, making effort, choosing an anchor—and then turns to the way those very movements can thicken the sense of a meditator who is managing it all. From there, the instruction opens toward a quieter register: choicelessness, receptivity, and a kind of surrender that doesn’t try to defeat the self so much as see through the habit of turning practice into a project. The guided sitting follows that arc, returning to breath, gentle effort, and the possibility of letting the doer relax.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from directing attention and making effort toward choicelessness, receptivity, and surrendering the sense of a doer.

chapters.title: Opening orientation
chapters.description: The talk begins by welcoming listeners and setting up the basic movement from speaking into guided practice.

chapters.title: Attention and effort
chapters.description: He names meditation as directing attention, making effort, and using intention, while noting how these moves can evoke self.

chapters.title: Selfing and practice
chapters.description: The teaching reframes self and not-self as different capacities, not opposites to win or lose, and prepares for the meditation instructions.

chapters.title: Settling with breath
chapters.description: Instructions turn to finding a sustainable posture, choosing an anchor, and gently returning attention to breathing and away from discursive thought.

chapters.title: Stability and reminders
chapters.description: He acknowledges ordinary choice-making and gives room for small reminders and support while stability develops.

chapters.title: Doer and deed
chapters.description: The meditation explores the natural sense of a meditator and object, then points to a deeper surrender that leaves less room for the doer to congeal.

chapters.title: Surrender to grace
chapters.description: Brensilver says surrender is not something the ego can accomplish by force, and invites a yielding to the Dharma's grace.

chapters.title: Beyond hierarchy
chapters.description: The closing repetition pushes beyond simple comparisons of self and not-self, warning that trying to rank them can strengthen the very selfing the practice is seeing.

title: Guided Meditation: Aperture, Vividness, Stability, Effort
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How can attention be held without turning meditation into another project of fixing, improving, or solving? The talk moves from intention—putting down greed and distress with respect to the world—into a very practical map of attention: where to place it, how wide to make the aperture, how to nurture vividness and stability, and how to adjust effort so it stays sustainable. After the instructions, the guided meditation settles into breath, body, and sound, then gradually opens into a more holistic way of noticing what is present.
short_summary: A guided meditation that works with intention, attention, vividness, stability, and sustainable effort, moving from a narrow anchor to a more open awareness.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the sit and frames the talk around a more concrete set of instructions than usual.

chapters.title: Intention to release
chapters.description: He emphasizes putting down greed and distress, and letting go of the urge to use meditation for problem-solving.

chapters.title: Attention and aperture
chapters.description: The guidance turns to where attention rests, including the metaphor of narrowing or widening the aperture of awareness.

chapters.title: Vividness and stability
chapters.description: He describes cultivating clear, vivid contact with experience while keeping attention steady.

chapters.title: Effort in balance
chapters.description: Brensilver explores the difference between sustainable effort and effort driven by clinging or control.

chapters.title: Settling into the sit
chapters.description: The guided portion begins by reassuring listeners that this period need not solve anything, and by softening the drive to optimize.

chapters.title: Anchor and soundscape
chapters.description: He invites attention to the breath, body, or soundscape, and stresses a gentle, sustainable way of staying with the object.

chapters.title: Returning from mind wandering
chapters.description: The meditation widens to include the mind’s activity, with instruction on noticing when agency collapses into thought and then reestablishing intimacy with the anchor.

chapters.title: Open awareness
chapters.description: The practice shifts from a concrete anchor to a more spacious noticing where the whole field of experience can be included.

title: Guided Meditation: What's Already Here?
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk opens with a simple but unsettling question: if peace and war can both come from the same human capacities, where do we actually turn to find freedom? From there, the speaker uses Ajahn Chah’s line about freedom and suffering arising in the same place to shift the whole meditation toward immediate experience. The guided practice then keeps returning to a quiet invitation: stop searching, let the breath come to you, and rest in what is already here. Over time, the sense of looking, strain, and self-doubt is meant to soften into being found by silence.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from the question of peace and war into a practice of letting the breath and silence find you rather than searching for something elsewhere.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: He introduces the sit with a brief reflection on war, cooperation, and the possibility that peace must be intentionally invented.

chapters.title: Freedom in suffering
chapters.description: Ajahn Chah's teaching frames the practice: freedom is found in the same place as suffering, so the invitation is to turn toward what's already here.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins with a sustainable posture and a deliberate shift away from efforting.

chapters.title: Letting breath arrive
chapters.description: He repeatedly invites the listener not to search for the breath, but to let the breath find them and suffuse the body.

chapters.title: Being found
chapters.description: The emphasis deepens into a release of duality and self-doubt as the experience of being found softens the sense of a seeker.

chapters.title: Resting in presence
chapters.description: He returns to the theme that freedom and suffering share the same place, encouraging the body to embody that freedom rather than pursue it.

chapters.title: Healing the search
chapters.description: The practice closes by resting in what is already here, with stillness and silence offering a gentle healing of fundamental alienation.

title: Guided Meditation: Faith in Silence
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if the mind’s constant thinking is just a form of coping, and the practice is to stop feeding it for a while? This guided meditation invites a gradual turn toward silence, with reassurance that letting go is not neglect and that the present moment does not wish to hurt you. The talk moves through settling the posture, softening the body, and resting attention in tranquility while exploring a faith that it is safe enough to release effort. Over and over, the instruction returns to listening to silence, trusting it, and letting the heart do what thought cannot.
short_summary: A guided meditation on loosening coping habits and trusting silence as a safe place to let go.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He opens with the framing of the practice, naming coping and inviting a posture that can be sustained.

chapters.title: Soothing the Body
chapters.description: The practice begins with embodied easing, fuller breaths, and softening tension in the face and belly.

chapters.title: Putting Down Complications
chapters.description: He reassures listeners that it is not neglect to set aside problems for a while and rest in tranquility.

chapters.title: Faith in Silence
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward trusting silence, letting go, and noticing that the present moment is not trying to harm us.

chapters.title: Over-Reading Experience
chapters.description: He points to the habit of making too much meaning out of sensations and phenomena, then invites a brief stopping.

chapters.title: Heart Training
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes how innocent the mind’s scrambling can be, and how much the heart can be trained.

chapters.title: Listening To Silence
chapters.description: A long guided period follows, in which listeners are asked to listen to silence, take cues from it, and keep letting go.

chapters.title: Extended Quiet
chapters.description: The meditation continues into a sustained stretch of silence with only faint, unclear audio artifacts near the end.

title: Guided Meditation: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins by asking for a posture of receptivity: to listen deeply enough that experience is received rather than turned into identity or resistance. From there it keeps circling the small ways meditation becomes a project of me—wanting, bargaining, control—and notices how that tightness relaxes when awareness is allowed to stay with breath, body, sound, and feeling. Near the end, a question about whether the agenda is to connect or to control opens into a quieter kind of faith: the sense that something can hold us even when our strategies fall away.
short_summary: A lightly guided meditation on receiving experience, noticing the agendas of wanting and control, and learning to trust what remains when effort softens.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with posture and a simple invitation to become receptive to Dharma and to one’s own wisdom.

chapters.title: Deep Listening
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the idea that what is not received in awareness becomes identification, urging a listening that does not turn into resistance.

chapters.title: The Project of Me
chapters.description: Awareness notices the subtle tension of trying to make meditation happen and the transactional mind that issues demands to the moment.

chapters.title: Wanting and Agenda
chapters.description: The practice turns toward background wanting, bargaining with the present, and the way knowing becomes entangled with those agendas.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: He points toward a confidence that remains after effort and strategy dissolve, encouraging trust in awareness that does not depend on the self.

chapters.title: Control or Connection
chapters.description: Using Michelle McDonald’s question, he explores whether practice is driven by control or connection and suggests that release is possible.

chapters.title: Faith in Letting Go
chapters.description: The meditation closes with repeated reassurance that something can hold us beyond control, ending with a quiet emphasis on release and willingness.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention as Prayer
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when attention is taken all the way to its deepest point—past effort, past self-conscious control, and into something like prayer? The talk begins with a Simone Weil line and then unfolds into a guided meditation where body, breath, sound, and forgiveness are used to soften the sense of a meditator who is trying to manage experience. Again and again, the practice turns from clunky doing toward a quieter trust that awareness can find us, and that the usual grip of self can relax.
short_summary: A guided meditation on attention, devotion, and the gradual release of selfing into an awareness that feels more like prayer than control.

chapters.title: Attention as Prayer
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Simone Weil’s line and names it as the conceptual pivot for the meditation.

chapters.title: View and Devotion
chapters.description: He explains how wise view and attention converge, and how the practice takes on a devotional quality.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The guided practice begins with posture, relaxation, and a gentle anchoring in body, breath, or sound.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and Recommitment
chapters.description: He invites forgiveness for imperfection and then recommits attention to the sacred within ordinary practice.

chapters.title: Love in Disguise
chapters.description: Attention is described as a form of love that can appear as worry, protectiveness, or longing for safety.

chapters.title: Less Control, More Grace
chapters.description: The language turns toward the clunkiness of selfing and the felt shift from effortful directing to receptive awareness.

chapters.title: Letting Awareness Find Us
chapters.description: He returns repeatedly to the sense that the meditator can soften, making room for awareness that is not possessed.

chapters.title: Groundless and Held
chapters.description: The close names self as agenda and responsibility, then ends with trust that when we let go, something will catch us.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention and Sustaining Openness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: How do you train attention without letting awareness narrow into effort or problem-solving? This guided meditation keeps returning to that tension: directing the mind toward breath, body, and sound while practicing a wider, more receptive openness when intensity, discomfort, or the urge to fix things starts to take over. The movement is gentle and practical, treating small moments of collapse as the place to notice how openness can be re-established.
short_summary: A guided meditation on balancing focused attention with a broad, steady openness when intensity pulls the mind toward problem-solving.

chapters.title: Practice frame
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the distinction between focusing attention and staying open, and explains why the practice looks at small moments of collapse.

chapters.title: Settle into posture
chapters.description: He invites the sit, encourages a simple posture, and points toward trusting enough goodness to begin.

chapters.title: Intensity and collapse
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward how life’s intensity narrows awareness into problem-solving, and how to notice that movement gently.

chapters.title: Reverence for dukkha
chapters.description: He names the First Noble Truth and explores how attention to breath, body, and sound can make the mind less brittle.

chapters.title: Good enough awareness
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes patience, forgiveness, and a quieter relation to the urge to optimize or fix the moment.

chapters.title: Re-establish openness
chapters.description: He suggests returning to okayness, letting energy unwind, and rebuilding the open field before leaning again on an anchor.

chapters.title: Not solving
chapters.description: The closing reflection points to the simple relief of not solving everything and resting in awareness instead.

title: Guided Meditation: Establishing Tranquility
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What changes when the mind stops bracing and narrows its field of attention? This guided meditation begins by settling into silence and posture, then asks the listener to relax what can be relaxed and allow the remaining tension to stay without struggle. From there, the practice turns toward the breath as a small, steady refuge: softening the inner gaze, feeling nourished by breathing, and noticing how even a little tranquility changes the way experience appears. Near the end, the talk points to the Buddha’s teaching that mind shapes what is experienced, using that idea to underscore the shift in view that comes with calm.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling into silence, narrowing attention to the breath, and noticing how even a little tranquility changes perception.

chapters.title: Settling Into Silence
chapters.description: The meditation begins by settling into posture and listening for what silence can reveal instead of immediately telling the mind what to do.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Body
chapters.description: He invites a simple release of tension, while allowing whatever cannot relax yet to remain without struggle.

chapters.title: Tranquility Changes View
chapters.description: The talk opens out into the idea that even a little tranquility changes perspective, without making life's difficulties disappear.

chapters.title: Narrowing Attention
chapters.description: Attention is described as a spotlight: rather than rejecting experience, the practice makes room for a smaller field centered on the breath.

chapters.title: Faith in Silence
chapters.description: Matthew names the fatigue of constant problem-solving and invites a kind of non-worry, softening into a smaller aperture of attention.

chapters.title: Stabilizing the Inner Gaze
chapters.description: With eyes closed, the listener is guided to steady a gaze that often scans for threat and to feel nourished by breathing.

chapters.title: Tranquility as Refuge
chapters.description: He links hyperarousal and suffering to the need for refuge, then closes by citing the Buddha on how mind shapes experience.

chapters.title: Closing Bells
chapters.description: The meditation ends with bells, leaving time for the quieter state to be felt.

title: Guided Meditation - Deep Letting Go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: If this moment had to be lived for all eternity, what posture would the heart take? The guided meditation keeps returning to that question as it invites a deep letting go of self-hatred, self-view, self-love, and self-care, then opens into a felt sense of futurelessness. From there, the practice loosens the burden of being sandwiched between memory and possibility, and explores how clinging creates the very sense of time it seems to inhabit. It ends by putting down models of the self to discover what remains when reference points and hope-fear patterns are allowed to melt.
short_summary: A guided meditation that uses futurelessness and repeated letting go to soften clinging, time, and self-models.

chapters.title: Opening question
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a direct invitation to consider the heart’s posture if this moment were to last forever.

chapters.title: Letting go inwardly
chapters.description: The practice turns toward deep release, gently questioning self-hatred, self-view, self-love, and self-care as fixed positions.

chapters.title: Outside memory and future
chapters.description: Matthew introduces 'futurelessness' to point to the burden created by time and asks the listener to sense life between memory and possibility.

chapters.title: Time and clinging
chapters.description: He explores time as impermanence and notes that clinging and time reinforce each other through a subtle habit of reference.

chapters.title: Dropping reference points
chapters.description: The meditation repeatedly invites the listener to stop reiterating reference points such as me and you, self and world, past and future.

chapters.title: Becoming empty
chapters.description: The practice emphasizes emptiness as a way of not letting thoughts, feelings, and sensations strike or harden into the heart.

chapters.title: Hope and fear soften
chapters.description: Wanting and not wanting are treated as ways the present freezes, and awareness is used to melt the stiffness of hope and fear.

chapters.title: Putting down self-models
chapters.description: The close asks the listener to release all models of self and discover what remains when identity is no longer being assembled.

title: Guided Meditation - Mindfulness of Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: The talk begins with the rain as a way into practice: letting sound soothe the body, noticing thirst, and settling into the repeated instruction to let go. From there, it turns to feeling as the place where worry, longing, and the sense that something must happen all register first, before they harden into emotional stories. The speaker keeps returning to the felt sense in the body, showing how to stay with urgency without obeying it, and how met feeling can become a quieter relationship to experience. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from reading life as right or wrong to meeting feeling with enough wisdom that it opens into love.
short_summary: A guided meditation on mindfulness of feeling, using rain, breath, and bodily sensation to soften urgency and meet experience with equanimity and love.

chapters.title: Settling with Rain
chapters.description: The meditation opens by inviting the listener to rest with the sound of rain and begin softening into the body and the room.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: Repeated phrases of letting go create a long settling phase, with attention easing away from effort and toward simple presence.

chapters.title: Feeling as Dharma
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Buddha’s teaching that sight, sound, thought, and sensation all converge on feeling, making feeling the center of practice.

chapters.title: Body and Affect
chapters.description: He describes feeling as something grounded in the body, with pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral tones, often sensed through the front of the body or as a wider body-wide atmosphere.

chapters.title: The Inner Meter
chapters.description: After a silence, he explores how feeling can seem to tell us what is right or wrong in a moment and invites attention away from that judgment toward the feeling itself.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Soothing
chapters.description: Equanimity is presented as a changing relationship to experience, where we breathe into arousal or numbness and let the tides of feeling be felt without obeying them.

chapters.title: Anchors and Urgency
chapters.description: The practice widens to include breath, body, and soundscape as possible anchors while urgency, fidgeting, and anxious self-story are met with patient attention.

chapters.title: Listening Instead of Reacting
chapters.description: When feeling seems to demand action, the instruction is to respond gently and listen rather than immediately follow the impulse.

chapters.title: Feeling into Love
chapters.description: The close emphasizes that feeling met with wisdom and equanimity can open into love, peace, compassion, joy, and kindness.

title: Guided Meditation - Landing in the Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Stillness here is treated as a place where bracing, fear, and self-protection can show up, and the practice is to meet them gently rather than turn them into a story about the future. The talk moves from settling into the breath, to opening to sensations as they are, to noticing how thought tries to manage uncertainty and how easily attention can drift into narrative. It then widens into walking meditation, where the focus shifts to bodily movement, visual impressions, and the patient, almost invisible accumulation of mindfulness, with one reference to a classic teaching on the five predictable mind-body energies that can arise in retreat.
short_summary: A guided meditation that settles into breathing, then extends into walking practice, using fear, distraction, and boredom as part of the path rather than obstacles.

chapters.title: Settling the Heart
chapters.description: The meditation begins by noticing bracing or fear and inviting the body and heart to soften with kindness.

chapters.title: Thoughts as Predictions
chapters.description: He reframes discomfort and worry as predictions about the future, then returns attention to present-moment experience.

chapters.title: Body Aliveness
chapters.description: Attention widens to the changing sensations of the body, including the subtle blur of experience with eyes closed.

chapters.title: Finding the Breath
chapters.description: He gathers awareness around the sensations of breathing and encourages a precise, steady contact with inhale and exhale.

chapters.title: Returning Without Judgment
chapters.description: The practice deepens into letting the rest of experience arise in the background while gently returning from narrative to presence.

chapters.title: Retreat Mind States
chapters.description: He names common energies of sleepiness, restlessness, desire, aversion, and doubt, and describes retreat as a place where all moments can be held in awareness.

chapters.title: The Obstacle Is Path
chapters.description: Using a teaching attributed to the Buddha, he normalizes the full range of mind states and says what looks like interference is part of the path.

chapters.title: Walking and Momentum
chapters.description: He turns to walking meditation, explaining how to meet movement, boredom, and visual impressions with continuity, and how practice builds quietly over time.

chapters.title: Surrender and Simplicity
chapters.description: The talk ends with a plain invitation to surrender to the retreat’s logic and trust the long practice tradition behind it.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting In Care
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What does it mean to begin a meditation by letting the heart lead? The talk invites a simple opening: rest in some accessible form of love—gratitude, compassion, loving-kindness, joy, equanimity, even grief—and let that color the body and breath. From there, it explores how love can soften irritation, make the breath feel significant, and create a steadier container in which anger or aversion can be held without forcing them away.
short_summary: A guided meditation that begins with an accessible form of love and uses that care to soften the breath, the body, and whatever arises.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The talk opens with a brief welcome and a transition into stillness.

chapters.title: Begin With Love
chapters.description: He invites a starting point of love, gratitude, compassion, joy, equanimity, or grief as access to the heart.

chapters.title: Care Flavors Experience
chapters.description: He points out how love, like irritation or sadness, can color everything, even the breath.

chapters.title: Body Suffused With Care
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a more embodied sense of tenderness and aliveness spread through the body.

chapters.title: Freedom Still Beckons
chapters.description: He reflects on the promise that practice is possible in any moment and that love and freedom are not foreclosed.

chapters.title: Steadier Love
chapters.description: The meditation emphasizes letting love become less ambivalent and more stable, without needing force.

chapters.title: Holding What Is Not Love
chapters.description: When difficult states arise, they can be placed into a larger container of love rather than fought or suppressed.

chapters.title: Closing Wish
chapters.description: The meditation ends with a gentle blessing for freedom from suffering.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting In What We Cannot Stop
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling into a posture you can say yes to, then gently turns toward gratitude for being alive and conscious. From there, the talk explores the difference between the effort of directing attention and the quieter dimension of awareness that does not depend on will. The practice movement is to notice what is already happening, including how awareness blooms and collapses, and to rest in what cannot be stopped rather than trying to manage experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, soft effort, and resting in the aspect of awareness that does not depend on will.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with posture, settling, and a gentle invitation to arrive in the room.

chapters.title: Gratitude for Awareness
chapters.description: He turns toward gratitude for being alive and conscious, and toward the improbable conditions that allow awareness at all.

chapters.title: Effort and Ease
chapters.description: Brensilver distinguishes the practical effort of directing attention from the deeper ease of letting things be.

chapters.title: Awareness Beyond Will
chapters.description: He explores awareness as something that does not depend on will, cannot be forced, and does not cost attention in the usual way.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as Grace
chapters.description: The talk lingers on the sense that mindfulness blooms and collapses without being manufactured, revealing the limits of self-control.

chapters.title: Resting in What Cannot Stop
chapters.description: The meditation closes by inviting rest in the awareness that cannot be stopped and therefore does not need to be managed.

title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Moment Soften You
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What if the work of practice is less about forcing the mind into stillness and more about letting experience soften you? The talk moves through a guided meditation that alternates between easing in, landing with a breath, and gently meeting whatever is present—thought loops, anxiety, body sensations, and the places where the heart tightens. Rather than asking for special states, it keeps returning to the felt shift from clenching to release, and to the repeated practice of trusting the heart to stay available.
short_summary: A guided meditation on softening into the present moment, meeting thought, emotion, and sensation with a gentler, more open attention.

chapters.title: Arriving
chapters.description: A brief opening welcomes listeners and settles into the practice.

chapters.title: Entering the moment
chapters.description: He describes different ways of coming into presence, whether gradually or all at once.

chapters.title: Finding the breath
chapters.description: The meditation turns to the breath as a way into immediacy, sometimes through effort and sometimes by simple availability.

chapters.title: Meeting the body
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to sense what hurts, what needs care, and how thought, anxiety, and emotion can be met with blessing.

chapters.title: Softened by phenomena
chapters.description: He broadens the practice to include all phenomena, treating thinking, sensation, and breath as conditions that can soften the heart.

chapters.title: When suppleness is lost
chapters.description: The talk acknowledges how identification and self-protective reactivity harden the heart, and how softness can return after contraction.

chapters.title: Letting go in practice
chapters.description: He lingers on the movement from clinging to release, including the way discomfort and relief can coexist in softening.

chapters.title: Rehearsing freedom
chapters.description: The closing frames practice as repeated rehearsal of freedom, like an athlete practicing a shot, without needing to force a result.

title: Guided Meditation: Renouncing the Narrative Impulse
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when feeling arises and the mind immediately turns it into a story, a diagnosis, or a command? This guided meditation keeps returning to the body and the feeling tone itself, inviting the listener to notice mood, arousal, and the urge to narrate without following that impulse. Over time, the practice softens the compulsive push to figure things out and opens a little space where nothing needs to happen right now.
short_summary: A guided meditation on staying with feeling without turning it into narrative, so the body can be met with less compulsive grasping.

chapters.title: Opening and Welcome
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a brief welcome and settling into the shared sit.

chapters.title: Inviting Goodwill
chapters.description: He turns attention toward the guided meditation and the sense of collective goodwill and gratitude.

chapters.title: Landing in the Body
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes breathing, arriving in the body, and becoming available to the present moment.

chapters.title: Feeling Without Story
chapters.description: He points to the body's feeling-circuits and explains the practice of letting feeling remain feeling without adding narrative.

chapters.title: Narrative and Vigilance
chapters.description: The talk names the natural habit of interpreting feeling through threat, opportunity, longing, and aversion, and the vulnerability of renouncing that habit.

chapters.title: Experimenting with Defenselessness
chapters.description: The meditation asks for direct contact with feeling, softening compulsive energies and the felt need to make something happen.

chapters.title: Nothing Needs to Happen
chapters.description: He guides a sustained period of resting with experience, opening to the possibility that nothing is required in this moment.

chapters.title: Distilling Wisdom
chapters.description: The closing notes suggest that this way of meeting feeling helps separate wisdom from compulsivity and supports living well.

title: Guided Meditation: Not updating your autobiography
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: What happens when meditation stops reinforcing the story you’ve been carrying about yourself? This guided practice keeps returning to the body and the breath, not to make pain pleasant, but to meet experience more precisely and let awareness spread through the whole field of sensation. From there, the talk turns toward surrender, equanimity, and not treating the present moment as the next chapter in an old autobiography.
short_summary: A guided meditation on dropping the autobiography and meeting experience through the body, breath, and equanimity.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes the group and begins the meditation with simple settling instructions.

chapters.title: Body and Breath
chapters.description: He orients attention to the felt sense of the body and breath, not trying to make pain pleasant but to include it more fully.

chapters.title: Awareness Spreads
chapters.description: The practice continues with repeated invitations to sit and let awareness fill out the body, soothing hot spots where possible.

chapters.title: Not the Next Chapter
